segments.s245-g201: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. The verb بشنید is simple past 'heard' with خسرو as its subject (poetic word order places the subject after the verb); از گیو marks the source/agent 'from Giv', and سخن is the object 'the words/speech'.
segments.s245-g202: بدو گفت is literally 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'); گفت here is the past-report verb 'he said' leading into direct speech. مندیش is an imperative/prohibitive sense ('do not worry/think' — an archaic negative imperative form); و connects two imperatives; زاری مکن is 'do not lament', where مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'.
segments.s245-g203: که here introduces the content clause 'that ...' (reporting the reason/content). بجانست is a fused adjectival/verb phrase (ب + جان + است) meaning 'is alive / dear to life'; خرسند باش is an imperative 'be glad' with باش as the 2nd‑person imperative.
segments.s245-g204: بر امید is a prepositional expression 'in/on the hope (of)'; گم بوده is a participial/reduced clause 'having been lost' (بوده = 'been'), modifying فرزند 'child'; باش is again the imperative 'be' (i.e. 'be hopeful').
segments.s245-g205: که introduces an explanatory/reporting clause ('for' / 'that'). ایدون is an adverbial/temporal word (here 'now' / 'recently' — an archaic usage); شنیدستم is first‑person past 'I heard' or 'I have heard' (the enclitic ـم marks 'I'); از موبدان gives the source 'from the mōbeds (priests)'.
segments.s245-g206: ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from'; بیدار دل is a compound epithet 'awake/clear‑hearted' (بیدار modifies دل); نامور further qualifies them as 'renowned', and بخردان is the plural noun 'wise men' (ب‑خرد‑ان, 'the wise'). The sequence lists qualities/sources describing those who reported it.
segments.s245-g207: که again introduces the clause 'that ...'. من is the subject 'I'; با سواران ایران is a comitative phrase 'with the horsemen of Iran'; بجنگ is the infinitival/subjunctive form 'to fight / to make war', which will be completed by the verb شوم in the following segment.
segments.s245-g208: سوی شهر توران gives the direction 'toward the city of Turan'. شوم is 1st‑person subjunctive/optative ('may I become' / here 'I will go / I shall set out'), used with the prior verbal phrase to express the speaker's intended action; بی‌درنگ is an adverb 'without delay / immediately' modifying the whole clause.
segments.s245-g129: پی is an adverbial/prepositional particle meaning 'in pursuit/after'. اندر + گرفتم forms the verbal phrase 'I took up' or 'I pursued' (گرفتم = past 'I took/seized'); the direct/locational complement 'همه دشت و کوه' ('all plains and mountains') follows and is modified by همه 'all'. Word order is emphatic/poetic: adverbial (پی) + directional/locative particle (اندر) + verb + its object.
segments.s245-g130: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'). از + تاختن is a prepositional phrase 'from galloping' giving the cause. شد is the past of شدن 'became' (inchoative), and سمندم is 'my steed' (سمند + possessive -م). ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary', so the clause reads 'for/from galloping my steed became weary.'
segments.s245-g131: ز بیژن is a prepositional phrase 'of/from Bijan' (poetic z = 'concerning/of'). ندیدم is the negative past 'I did not see.' بجایی here is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in any place/anywhere' (usage in the poem), and نشان is the direct object 'a sign/trace'. The order places the negated verb before the location phrase for emphasis.
segments.s245-g132: جزین 'except this' limits the preceding negation. اسب 'horse' and زین 'saddle' are the items excepted. از پس 'from behind/after' (از = from, پس = after/behind) + ایدر 'here' gives the place/source, and کشان is a participial/relational form 'dragging' (used adjectivally) describing how they are present: 'dragged here from behind.'
segments.s245-g133: دلم is 'my heart' (دل + possessive -م). شد is 'became.' پر آتش is a compound predicative 'full of fire' (literally 'full (پر) of fire/ardor'), and ز تیمار اوی is a causal phrase 'from/ because of (ز) the care/concern (تیمار) of/for him' (اوی = his/her/him as possessive/3rd-person clitic).
segments.s245-g134: که begins an explanatory clause ('for/since'). چون here functions causally/as 'as/that' leading into the clause. بود is past 'was.' با گور پیکار اوی: با = 'with', گور = 'gūr' (here 'onager/wild ass'), پیکار = 'battle', and اوی is the 3rd-person clitic, giving the sense 'his battle' or indicating he is the one engaged — together: 'for he was engaged in battle with the onager.'
segments.s245-g135: بماندم is the 1st-person past 'I remained/stayed' with the prefix ب- marking continuative aspect. فراوان functions adverbially 'for a long time/abundantly.' بر آن مرغزار is a locative phrase 'on that meadow' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, مرغزار = meadow).
segments.s245-g136: همی is an emphatic/continuous adverb 'continually/still.' کردمش is a compound of کردَن + object-clitic -ش + 1st-person ending -م: literally 'I did it/him' — here idiomatically 'I sought him' (the -ش refers back to the person sought). هر سوی is 'every direction' (هر = every, سوی = direction/toward), and خواستار functions as 'seeking/desirous', so the clause means 'I kept seeking him in every direction.'
segments.s245-g177: A string of names/titles and adjectives in vocative position: the proper name 'انوشه' is followed by the title 'جهاندار' ('lord/sovereign'), then the adjective 'نیک' modifying the following noun 'اخترا'. Together they form an epithet addressed to the addressee. The order (name → title → adjective → noun) is characteristic of poetic epithets.
segments.s245-g178: 'نبینی' here is a 2nd-person present negative interrogative/exhortative ('do you not see...?'). 'که' introduces the subordinate content ('what'), and 'بر سر چه آمد' is a fixed past‐tense verbal phrase meaning 'what came upon (one's) head' = 'what befell (one)'. The past verb 'آمد' is third person past; the clitic sequence 'م ... را' represents the object 'مرا' ('me') attached after the verb for emphasis/closure.
segments.s245-g179: A compact predication: the prepositional/relational 'ز گیتی' ('of/from the world') followed by 'یکی پور' ('one/certain son') and the past copula 'بودم' ('I was'), with the predicate adjective 'جوان' ('young' / 'young man') placed after the verb. Poetically 'ز' = 'of/from' marks origin (i.e. 'a son of the world').
segments.s245-g180: 'شب و روز' is the fixed adverbial phrase 'night and day'. 'بودم' (past 'I was') follows, and the compound 'بدوبر نوان' qualifies the state: 'بدوبر' ('twofold/ double') modifies 'نوان' ('youthful/new'), so together they mean something like 'in double youth' or 'renewed in two ways'. Word order places the temporal phrase first, then the verb, then the modifying adjective phrase.
segments.s245-g181: 'بجانش' ('به جانش' contracted) is an oathful/affective prepositional phrase literally 'by his life' used to introduce the emotional statement. 'پر از بیم' = 'full of fear/anxiety' (پر + از + noun). 'گریان' is the participial/adjectival form 'weeping' and 'بدم' is an archaic first-person past copula meaning 'I was' (here forming 'I was weeping'). The sequence sets cause/manner before the verbal predicate.
segments.s245-g182: 'ز درد' ('from the pain') gives the cause, 'جداییش' is 'his/its separation' (جدا + possessive clitic یش). 'بریان' is an adjectival/participle form meaning 'burnt/consumed' (often figurative: consumed by grief), and again 'بدم' is the archaic 'I was' closing the clause. The clause literal order is cause → affected noun → predicate adjective → copula.
segments.s245-g183: 'کنون آمد' is a simple past narrative ('now came' / 'presently came'). 'ای شاه گرگین' is the vocative 'O king Gorgin' (note the epithet/name sequence), and 'ز راه' ('from the road') indicates the source/direction of his coming. The verb precedes the vocative and the locative phrase follows as adverbial.
segments.s245-g184: Two parallel noun phrases describing him: 'زبان پر ز یافه' = 'a tongue full of یافه (acquired/obtained matters or artifice)' where 'پر' ('full of') follows the noun and 'ز یافه' is the complement; and 'روان پر گناه' = 'a soul/mind full of sin'. The construction repeats the pattern (noun → پر → از/ز → modifier) for rhetorical balance.
segments.s245-g145: "چو" is a temporal subordinating particle meaning "when" introducing the clause. "فرزند را گیو" is a topical/ appositive sequence: "فرزند را" (the son, with را marking definiteness/emphasis) is followed by the name "گیو" in apposition. "گم بوده" is a stative past (been/was lost) with بوده + adjectival/participial sense, and "دید" is the main perceptual verb "saw/perceived" whose subject is the narrator/agent; the whole segment gives the time/background for what follows.
segments.s245-g146: "سخن را" (the speech/words, with را marking definiteness/affectedness) is the direct theme, "برآنگونه" is an adverbial phrase "in that way/thereupon," and "آلوده دید" is a predicative phrase meaning "he saw (it) tainted/soiled." The second "دید" repeats the perceptual verb to assert how he judged the speech; overall this clause reports his perception of the words as corrupted.
segments.s245-g147: "ببرد" here is a past/narrative verb meaning "carried off / took away." The agent "اهرمن" (Ahriman/devil) precedes the object phrase "گیو را دل" (Giv’s heart, with را marking the definite/affected object) and "ز جای" means "from its place/seat" — together an idiom meaning Ahriman stole or stole away Giv's courage/heart.
segments.s245-g148: "همی خواست" is a habitual/intentional past (he wished/was inclined) used in narrative to express desire. The clause expresses purpose: "کو را" is a relative/indefinite "who/whom" (archaic use; marked by را), and "درآرد ز پای" is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning "to bring down/overthrow (make fall from the feet)." In other words, he desired to overthrow someone; the verb "درآرد" is the purpose/complement of "خواست."
segments.s245-g149: "بخواهد" is a modal/future-like verb meaning "he will/would want/desire." "ازو" (from him/of him) marks the source, "کین" names the motive "vengeance," and "پور گزین" (literally "choose a son") states the object/action he would take — i.e. he intends to select a son from him as an instrument of vengeance.
segments.s245-g150: "وگر" introduces a concessive condition "but if / however if." "چند" here functions adverbially as "however much / however many" modifying "نیک آید" ("good comes"). "او را" topicalizes/marks the affected person "to him," and "ازین" (از + این) means "from this"; the clause sets up a concessive clause meaning "but if, however much good comes to him from this..." and is left anticipating the following comment.
segments.s245-g151: "پس" links the narration as "then/thereupon." "اندیشه کرد" is a simple past clause "he thought/pondered." The following infinitival/optative phrase "اندران بنگرید" (in these matters to look) expresses his resolution or attempt — the classical combination "کرد" + verb indicates he set about or intended to examine/inspect the matters "in them."
segments.s245-g152: "نیامد" is a negative past/result verb "did not come/occur." With the adverb "همی" (emphatic/continuous), the nominal "روشنایی" (light/clarity) and the verbal root "پدید" (appear/manifest), the clause states that no light/clarity manifested — i.e. his examination brought no illumination.
segments.s245-g9: همه بیشه برگشت و کس را ندید: همه ('the whole') modifies بیشه ('thicket'); برگشت is a past tense (3rd person) verb 'returned/roamed' used with an omitted subject. کس را uses کس in the sense 'no one' with the object marker را, and ندید is the negative past (did not see). Word order is typical older Persian: determiner + noun, verb, then object + را + negative past verb.
segments.s245-g10: نه نیز اندرو بانگ مرغان شنید: نه نیز ('nor/also not') continues the negation; اندرو is locative 'in/therein' (referring back to the thicket). بانگ مرغان is the object 'the cry/voices of the birds', and شنید is the simple past 'heard' (here negated by نه). The adverbial negative (نه نیز) precedes the clause, a common poetic order.
segments.s245-g11: همی گشت بر گرد آن مرغزار: همی before the verb gives a habitual or continuous past sense ('kept / continually'); گشت is the past of گشتن here meaning 'roamed / went about'. بر گرد is a prepositional phrase 'around', followed by the demonstrative آن and the noun مرغزار 'meadow'. The clause means he repeatedly moved about that meadow.
segments.s245-g12: همی یار کرد اندرو خواستار: همی again marks continuative action; یار ('companion') is the thing sought or desired, کرد is the past/light verb 'made/did' used here in a compound sense with یار (i.e. to seek or make a companion), اندرو is locative 'therein/there', and خواستار is an adjectival/participle 'desirous/seeking'. Overall the phrase expresses that he kept seeking (or trying to find) a companion there.
segments.s245-g13: یکایک ز دور اسب بیژن بدید: یکایک ('one by one' / 'one after another') is an adverbial modifier; ز دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar'; اسب بیژن ('Bijan's horse') is the direct object (no را is required in poetic prose), and بدید is the simple past 'saw'. The usual SOV order holds: adverbials + object + verb.
segments.s245-g14: که آمد ازان مرغزاران پدید: که is a relative pronoun 'that/which'; آمد is past 'came'; ازان ('from those') is a poetic form of از آن, specifying source; مرغزاران is the plural 'meadows'; and پدید is a verb 'appeared/came into view'. The relative clause describes the horse as emerging from those meadows.
segments.s245-g15: گسسته لگام و نگون کرده زین: گسسته is a past participle 'broken' placed before لگام ('bridle'), giving 'the bridle broken'. The conjunction و links to نگون کرده زین, where نگون ('overturned/overthrown') with کرده (past participle 'made/done') modifies زین ('saddle'), yielding 'the saddle overturned'. The construction uses participial adjectives and a light verb (کرده) common in epic style.
segments.s245-g16: فرو مانده بر جای اندوهگین: فرو + مانده is a compound participial form meaning 'fallen down / remained fallen' (فرو intensifies downward motion, مانده = 'left/lying'); بر جای is locative 'on the spot', and اندوهگین is an adjective 'sorrowful'. The clause describes the (implied) subject as lying fallen at that place in sorrow.
segments.s245-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when/for'), here starting a past continuous description. یک هفته is a time expression 'for a week'. گرگین is the proper name Gorgin and is followed by the epithet بره‌بر ('lamb-bearer' / 'shepherd') in apposition. بپای is the verbal stem 'to lie in wait / keep watch'; here it appears before the auxiliary همی بود (in the next segment) and together they form a past continuous notion 'kept lying in wait.'
segments.s245-g2: همی بود is a past continuous/iterative construction ('was still / kept on being'); همی gives continuative emphasis and بود is the past of 'to be'. و simply links the clauses. بیژن نیامد is a straightforward past negative 'Bijan did not come.' بجای means 'in the place / back to his place' — together نیامد بجای = 'did not return.'
segments.s245-g3: ز هر سوش is a poetic locative phrase 'from every side/direction' (ز = 'from', هر = 'every', سوش = 'side/toward'). پویان here denotes the seekers/those who are seeking (a participial/agentive sense). بجستن گرفت is a periphrastic idiom where بجستن 'to seek' is followed by گرفت (literally 'took') functioning like 'began to' — 'began to search.' The verb گرفت marks the start of the action.
segments.s245-g4: رخان را is a marked direct object (را = object marker). بخوناب is a compound/adverbial meaning 'in/with blood-water' (instrumental/descr.). شستن is the infinitive/masdar 'to wash' and گرفت again serves as the auxiliary meaning 'began to' or 'took to' (i.e., they began washing). Word order puts the object before the verbal phrase, typical Persian ordering when را is present.
segments.s245-g5: پشیمانی is the noun 'remorse/regret' serving as subject. آمدش is the past verb آمد 'came' combined with the enclitic ش = 'to him' (dative/benefactive), so 'remorse came to him.' زان is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from that / for that reason' and کار خویش = 'his own deed/act' (کار = deed/affair, خویش reflexive 'his own'). The whole segment means 'remorse came to him for that deed of his.'
segments.s245-g6: که introduces the subordinate explanatory clause ('that/because'), and چون here functions as an evaluative 'how/that' (literary intensifier: 'in that he...'). بد is an adverb/adjective 'badly/ill' modifying سگالید (past 'set dogs upon' or 'sent dogs to hunt/assail'). بر is the preposition 'upon/against', and یار خویش = 'his friend/companion' (یار = companion, خویش with enclitic ش = 'his own'). The implied subject of سگالید is the same actor mentioned earlier ('he').
segments.s245-g7: بشد is an archaic/periphrastic past-of-be or 'it came to pass / they proceeded' used here to introduce the action (literal 'it became' → 'thereupon the Tazians acted'). تازیان is the subject 'the Tazians / Arabs.' تا ... indicates extent 'as far as / up to', and بدان جشنگاه is a fused locative phrase ('to that festive place' — ب + دان combine here as an older form of بدان). The whole means 'the Tazians went up to that place of festivity.'
segments.s245-g8: کجا is the relative/interrogative 'where' introducing the clause describing the place. بیژن گیو are two proper names (Bijan and Giv) functioning as the subject (joint). گم کرد is a compound past meaning 'lost' (گم = 'lost/astray', کرد used as the auxiliary 'did/made' forming the verbal phrase), and راه is the direct object 'way/path.' Word order is typical Persian relative clause: place-word (کجا) + subject + verbal phrase + object.
segments.s245-g137: ازو (from him) precedes the verb as a prepositional phrase; باز گشتم is a verbal phrase where باز functions adverbially as “back/again” and گشتم is the past-tense 1st/3rd‑person form of گشتن used here as “I/ he turned/returned.” چنین ناامید is a short predicative phrase (“thus hopeless”) modifying the subject (implied speaker or protagonist). Word order (preposition + adverb + verb + predicative) is typical of classical poetry.
segments.s245-g138: که introduces a subordinate (explanatory/cause) clause (‘that/for’). گور ژیان is a nominal phrase in which ژیان (life/the living) qualitatively specifies گور (grave) — i.e. “a grave of the living” (a ‘living grave’). بود is the past copula (“was”), and the coordinated predicate continues after و with دیو سپید (“a white demon”), so the clause says it was both a living-grave and a white demon (two predicates applied to an implied subject).
segments.s245-g139: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بشنید is the past‑tense verb “he heard.” The proper name گیو follows the verb (common in Persian poetic word order). این سخن is the direct object “this speech.” هوشیار is an appositive/adjectival epithet attached to Giv (‘the alert one’) rather than a separate verb.
segments.s245-g140: بدانست is a past‑tense verb meaning ‘he perceived/understood.’ کو را here functions as a fused interrogative/relative object meaning ‘who/which’ (classical usage); because that particular sense is not listed in the provided senses for کو, it is marked as unknown. را is the definite/object marker attached to کو, and تباهست is an archaic fused form = تباه است (‘is ruined’), with کار the noun ‘affair/work’ as the complement: ‘he understood who/whose work was ruined.’
segments.s245-g141: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of’ marking source (here introducing the speaker of the words). گرگین is the proper name Gorgin (not an adjective) and is the source/agent of the سخن (speech/words). سربسر is an adverb meaning ‘entirely/throughout,’ خیره modifies دید to give the sense ‘stared intently,’ and دید is the past verb ‘he saw/he gazed.’ The order places the source and object before the adverbial and the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s245-g142: همی is a continuous adverb (‘ever/continually’). چشمش is the possessed noun ‘his eye(s)’ (subject of دید). از روی او is a prepositional phrase that indicates source/target — literally ‘from/upon his face’ or ‘at the sight of him’ — and تیره is a predicative adjective ‘dark/bleak’; دید is the past verb ‘saw.’ The clause reads literally ‘his eye continually saw (his) face dark,’ i.e. his eyes perceived his countenance as dark or his face became dark before his eyes.
segments.s245-g143: رخش here denotes ‘countenance/appearance’ (not the horse) and is the noun that becomes زرد (‘pale/wan’). از بیم is a causal prepositional phrase ‘because of/from fear,’ where از has the sense ‘because of’ and بیم means ‘fear.’ سالار شاه is a genitival sequence ‘the commander of the king’ (the one feared). The whole phrase says literally ‘his face/countenance pale because of fear of the king’s commander.’
segments.s245-g144: سخن here is ‘speech/words’ and is described by the adjective/participle لرزلرزان (‘trembling, shaking’). و links the coordinate phrase دل پر گناه: دل ‘heart’ as the noun, پر used adjectivally as ‘full (of),’ and گناه ‘sin/guilt’ as the complement — together ‘his words trembled, and his heart was full of guilt.’ This is a coordinate description of his inner and outward state.
segments.s245-g97: برفتیم is past 1st-person plural (“we went/advanced”) with the prefixed بر- common in classical/poetic Persian. ز ایدر is a short prepositional phrase: ز (poetic از) “from” + ایدر “here.” بجنگ is the infinitive/ purpose-marker “to fight” (in classical poetry the infinitive can follow without an additional preposition) and takes گراز (“the boar”) as its object; the whole phrase means “we went from here to fight the boar.”
segments.s245-g98: رسیدیم is past 1st-person plural “we arrived/reached.” نزدیک is an adverb/adjectival preposition “near.” ارمان فراز here functions as a place-name or a proper-name phrase following نزدیک; classical poetry often places such names without an ezāfe. Because ارمان and فراز together serve as a toponym in context, they are treated as a name rather than their ordinary lexical senses.
segments.s245-g99: یکی بیشه is a noun phrase “a certain grove” (یکی = “a certain”). دیدیم is the main verb “we saw.” کرده is a past participle used here to describe the state or making of the grove (“made/formed” or “left in that state”), and چو introduces a simile “as/like,” with دست (“hand”) as the image compared to the trees’ truncated branches.
segments.s245-g100: درختان بریده is a noun + participle sequence “trees cut off” (describing the grove’s condition). چراگاه پست is a descriptive apposition “a low pasture” (پست = “low”) giving the character of that place; classical Persian often strings such noun phrases without an explicit copula or ezāfe.
segments.s245-g101: همه جای is “every place” / “everywhere.” گشته is the perfect/verb meaning “has become” or “turned (into).” کنام گراز is a genitival phrase functioning as predicate complement: کنام (“bosom/seat/haunt”) + گراز “of the boar,” so the clause means “every place has become the boar’s lair.”
segments.s245-g102: همه شهر ارمان is “the whole city of Arman” (همه as determiner + شهر + proper name ارمان). از آن is a causal phrase “because of that” (از used in a causal sense), در کزاز is a state “in kazzāz” — kزاز literally ‘tetanus’ but here describes a convulsive or panic-stricken condition; the phrase says the city of Arman was thrown into that condition by what had happened.
segments.s245-g103: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” ما is the subject “we.” جنگ را with the object-marker را marks the martial purpose/occasion (“for the fight”). نیزه برگاشتیم is a compound verbal action: نیزه “spear” + برگاشتیم (past 1pl with بر- indicating an upward or taking-up action) meaning “we took up/raised our spears.” The whole clause reads “When we took up spears for the fight…”
segments.s245-g104: ببیشه is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/into the grove” (ب- prefixed to بیشه). درون is an adverb “within/inside.” بانگ برداشتیم is an idiomatic verbal phrase “we raised a cry” (بانگ “cry/shout” + برداشتیم ‘we lifted/raised’); classical Persian commonly uses برداشتن in the sense “to raise (a sound, cry).”
segments.s245-g17: بدانست is a past-tense, narrative verb 'he realized/knew' placed before the relative clause; کو را introduces the person (archaic use of کو ~ 'who'), with را functioning as the Persian object/definiteness marker attached to that element. تباهست is a predicative adjective/compound meaning 'is ruined' and کار is the noun 'affair/deed' — together 'کو را تباهست کار' = 'whose affair is ruined'. Word order is typical older Persian: main verb (بدانست) + relative phrase.
segments.s245-g18: بدین روزگار is a temporal phrase 'in these days' (بـ + این + روزگار; here the input tokens are split oddly). نیاید is a negative-present/present-general verb 'does not come/does not befall' placed before the temporal phrase for emphasis; the whole clause states that such a thing 'does not befall Iran in these times.'
segments.s245-g19: This is a conditional listing: اگر ... اگر ... pairs two protases ('if there is ... if there is ...'). دار here means 'gallows' (an idiomatic item in such lists), دارد is the present/past-narrative 'has/exists', and چاه و بند are additional nouns 'pit/well and fetter(s)'. The repeated اگر is rhetorical; the sequence enumerates possible instruments of punishment or restraint.
segments.s245-g20: از + name + آمدستش + گزند is a simple verbless-turned-verb clause in which آمدستش (poetic contracted past) means 'befell him' or 'came upon him' and گزند is the affected noun 'harm'. از افراسیاب indicates the source/agent 'from Afrasiab'. Word order is source — verb — patient.
segments.s245-g21: کمند اندرافگند is a compact verbal phrase: کمند 'the snare' is the subject and اندرافگند (poetic perfective) 'threw/cast into' is the verb. The conjunction و links the next clause; برگاشت (past narrative) 'he turned/raised' and روی 'his face' completes the action 'he turned his face.' The verbs are past narrative forms typical of epic storytelling.
segments.s245-g22: ز کرده پشیمان is an ablative-phrase 'regretful because of the deed' (ز = 'from/because of', کرده = 'the thing done/deed', پشیمان = 'repentant'). و links the next coordinated phrase دل جفت جوی: دل 'heart' + جفت جوی (a compound) 'seeking a mate/companion' where جفت is 'pair/companion' and جوی is a deverbal/noun 'seeker/searcher' — overall 'his heart yearning for a companion.' Word order is noun + modifying noun/participle.
segments.s245-g23: ازان مرغزار is a locative-ablative phrase 'from that meadow' (ازان = 'from that', مرغزار = 'meadow'). اسب بیژن براند is a past narrative clause where اسب is the direct object/vehicle 'his horse', بیژن the agent/name, and براند (narrative past of راندن) 'he rode/drove (it) away'. The verb follows the object in the classical narrative order.
segments.s245-g24: بخیمه در آورد is a light-verb construction: بخیمه (ب + خیمه) 'into the tent' (بـ = into/in) + آورد (past 'brought/placed') — 'brought (it) into the tent.' و links the final clause روزی بماند where بماند is the past narrative 'he remained/stayed'; روزی here refers to 'a day' (temporal duration). The sequence says he brought (the horse) into the tent and remained for a day.
segments.s245-g313: This clause describes the agent and action in compact poetic word order: 'یکی' (a certain one) is the indefinite subject, 'جام' (cup) the object, and the past participle 'نهاده' (placed/put) gives the main action. 'بر کف' (on the palm) is the locative phrase (preposition + noun). 'نبید' (wine) is postposed to specify the cup's contents (a common poetic appositive pattern: noun followed by its qualifier without an ezâfe).
segments.s245-g314: 'بدو' here means 'in/unto it' (referring back to the cup) and combines with 'اندرون' (within/inside) to give 'within it'. 'هفت کشور' is the noun phrase 'seven countries/realms' which is the theme of the verb. 'پدید' is a classical/poetic verb meaning 'appeared/manifested' and appears at the end as is normal in Persian; the overall construction means 'within it the seven countries appeared'.
segments.s245-g315: 'زمان و نشان' are the items being revealed ('time and sign/omen'). 'سپهر بلند' is the subject phrase 'the lofty/heavenly sky' (adjective follows noun as usual in Persian). The sequence is inverted for poetic effect: the things revealed are named first, then the revealer (the lofty sky).
segments.s245-g316: This clause contains the verb complex and the enumerated interrogative objects. 'همه' ('all') is the direct object, 'کرده' is the perfect/participle 'having made/done', and 'پیدا' ('apparent/visible') functions as predicate ('made all apparent'). The string 'چه و چون و چند' lists interrogatives 'what, how, and how many', explaining the contents that were revealed; Persian often strings such question-words in apposition after verbs of revelation.
segments.s245-g317: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'. 'ماهی' is used poetically (here as 'a moonlike/beautiful one' rather than literal fish) and 'بجام اندرون' functions as a compact locative 'in the cup' (in poetry the preposition and noun may fuse). 'تا' introduces the range ('up to/until') and 'بره' ('lamb') marks the other end of that range; together this gives 'from … in the cup up to the lamb', i.e. the catalogue of things that appeared. Note: the token 'بجام' in the word-list lacks a matching sense for 'in the cup' and so is marked -1 below.
segments.s245-g318: 'نگاریده' is a past participle 'painted/portrayed' modifying 'پیکر' ('figure/body'). 'همه یکسره' is an emphatic adverbial phrase meaning 'all entirely/wholly' (همه = all, یکسره = completely), so the clause says that every depicted figure was shown in full. Word order (participle + noun + intensifier) is typical for poetic Persian.
segments.s245-g319: 'چو' = 'like/as' introduces a simile; what follows is a list of proper names and a noun (astrological/astral names): 'کیوان' (Kayvân/Saturn), 'بهرام' (Bahram — here the martial/planetary sense is intended), 'ناهید' (Nâhid/Venus) and 'شیر' (the Lion/Leo). The conjunction 'و' links the items. In classical poetry such lists name celestial/astrological figures that are being likened.
segments.s245-g320: Again 'چو' begins another simile. 'خورشید' = the Sun. 'تیر' here is best read in its calendrical/astrological sense (the name of the month/constellation), and 'از بر' is a compact prepositional phrase meaning 'from above' (from/on the side of). The final phrase 'ماه زیر' means 'the Moon below' ('زیر' = under/beneath). The clause contrasts celestial influences above and below.
segments.s245-g193: This clause uses the poetic preposition ز ('because of/from') + noun phrase to give the cause: غمی شد ('a grief became' = 'he was filled with sorrow'). Word order is subjectless impersonal: غمی شد (lit. 'a grief occurred') followed by the cause ز درد دل گیو ('from the pain of Giv's heart'), where درد دل (pain of heart) is a genitive-like sequence and گیو names whose heart it is.
segments.s245-g194: This coordination links two reactions with و ('and'). برآشفت is a past/preterite poetic form meaning 'he became angry/was thrown into agitation'. بنهاد is the past of نهادن ('to place/put') used here in a literary register; it governs the following object فرخ کلاه ('fortunate/lucky cap'), i.e. 'he put on/placed the lucky cap'.
segments.s245-g195: رخ شاه ('the king's face/countenance') is a possessive noun phrase. بر گاه ('on the occasion/at that moment') is an adverbial phrase placed after the possessed noun to mark time. بی‌رنگ شد is the intransitive past construction 'became pale/colorless' (بی‌رنگ + شد).
segments.s245-g196: This causal clause begins with ز ('from/because of') followed by تیمار بیژن ('care/concern for Bijan'); دلش تنگ شد is an idiomatic perfective sequence meaning 'his heart became tight' or 'he felt anxious/sorrowful', with the enclitic pronoun -ش marking 'his'.
segments.s245-g197: بگیو here is a contraction of به + گیو ('to Giv') and introduces the indirect object (address) rather than a verb; آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. گفت is the past verb 'said' introducing Gorgin's speech, گرگین is the speaker's name. The following چه گفت repeats the question 'what did he say?'.
segments.s245-g198: This line contains a series of rhetorical questions. چه گوید uses the present/subjunctive form گوید to mean 'what can he say / what would he say' (a literary present used for speculation). کجا ماند ('where remains/was left') asks location or survival, and از نیک جفت literally 'of/from a good mate/pair' questions where any virtuous match remains. The clauses are elliptical and rhetorical.
segments.s245-g199: ز گفتار گرگین ('from/after Gorgin's speech') uses ز + noun to indicate source or cause; پس آنگاه ('then, thereafter') is a pleonastic pair of temporal adverbs emphasizing sequence, and گیو is the subject 'Giv' who acts next.
segments.s245-g200: سخن گفت is the idiom 'to speak' (سخن = speech, گفت = said/spoke); با خسرو ('with Khosrow') marks the addressee. از پور نیو introduces the topic 'about/ concerning Pour Niu' (پور as 'son/descendant' or element of a name, here part of the name phrase); the preposition از marks that the speech concerns that person.
segments.s245-g105: گراز اندر آمد = a nominal subject + motion verb: 'گراز' (the boar/boars) is followed by the locative particle 'اندر' (in/into) and the past verb 'آمد' (came/entered). 'بکردار کوه' is a poetic, contracted phrase meaning 'به‌کردارِ کوه' (in the shape/appearance of a mountain); here the prepositional/qualifying 'به' is attached as the prefix 'بـ-'.
segments.s245-g106: نه یک‌یک بهر جای گشته گروه = 'نه' negates the following phrase; 'یک‌یک' is the adverbial repetition 'one by one'; 'بهر جای' = 'for/every place' ('بهر' toward/for + 'جای' place); 'گشته' is the perfect/participial form of شدن meaning 'has become / were placed/turned' and governs 'گروه' (group/troop), so the sense is 'they were not single but had become groups occupying each place.'
segments.s245-g107: بکردیم جنگی بکردار شیر = 'بکردیم' is 1st‑person plural past 'we did/made'; its object is 'جنگی' (a fight/war). 'بکردار شیر' again is the poetic 'به‌کردارِ شیر' — 'in the manner/guise of a lion' — so the whole phrase means 'we waged a war in lion‑like fashion' or 'we fought like a lion.'
segments.s245-g108: بشد روز و نامد دل از جنگ سیر = 'بشد' (it became/it happened) governs the coordinated nouns 'روز و نامد' (day and renown/fame) — i.e. 'the day dawned and fame came.' The clause 'دل از جنگ سیر' is elliptic (copula implied): 'دل (شد) از جنگ سیر' = 'the heart became/was sated with war.' 'سیر' is predicative 'sated/filled.'
segments.s245-g109: چو پیلان بهم بر فگندیمشان = 'چو' = 'when/as'; 'پیلان' is the plural 'elephants'; 'بهم' = 'together/at one another'; 'بر' = 'against/on'; 'فگندیمشان' is 1st‑person plural past 'we threw' with the object clitic '-شان' ('them'). The sequence expresses a temporal clause: 'When we hurled the elephants against one another...' Note the archaic past-stem 'فگند' (throw).
segments.s245-g110: بمسمار دندان بکندیمشان = 'بمسمار' = 'with a nail/with a spike' (instrumental 'بـ' + 'مسمار'); 'دندان' here means 'tusk'; 'بکندیمشان' is 1st‑person plural past 'we tore/broke them away' with the object clitic '-شان'. Together: 'we tore out their tusks with spikes.'
segments.s245-g111: وزآنجا بایران نهادیم روی = 'وزآنجا' = 'and from there/thereupon'; 'بایران' is used as a locative/directional noun meaning 'to the barren/waste (lands)' (here an objective place); 'نهادیم' is 1st‑person plural past of نهادن 'we placed/set/laid' and with 'روی' ('face/direction') means 'we set our face/took our course toward ...'.
segments.s245-g112: همه راه شادان و نخچیر جوی = 'همه راه' = 'the whole way/all the way'; 'شادان' = 'joyful, rejoicing' (adverbial/qualifying the travel); 'نخچیر جوی' is a compound where 'نخچیر' = 'spoil/prey' (booty) and 'جوی' = 'seeking' (from 'جستن') — together 'seeking spoils'. The line describes traveling off happily while in search of booty.
segments.s245-g289: بکام تو بادا is an optative formula: بکام (’to one's desire’) with the possessive تو; بادا is the optative particle ‘may it be’. The following noun سپهر بلند (‘lofty heavens/sky’) is the thing wished to be 'to your liking' (word order in classical Persian can place the wish before the noun it governs).
segments.s245-g290: This is a negative optative clause. بجان تو literally ‘by/for your life’ (an idiomatic locus), هرگز ‘never’, and مبادا is the negative optative ‘may it not be’. گزند is the noun ‘harm’; the clause means ‘may harm never come upon your life’. Note the classical ordering where the negative optative follows the temporal/possessive phrase.
segments.s245-g291: ز نیکی دهش ‘a gift/grant from goodness’ is a prepositional phrase (ز = ‘from’). بر تو باد آفرین uses باد as the optative ‘may’ and آفرین as ‘praise’/‘acclamation’ — literally ‘may praise be upon you’ (بر تو = upon you). The construction is a conventional benediction.
segments.s245-g292: که introduces a result/explanatory clause: ‘that/for’. بر تو = ‘upon you’. برازد is a classical third-person present meaning ‘befits / is fitting for’ (here ‘suits you’). کلاه و نگین are the items that ‘befit’ the addressee; note the adjective-like use of برازد as a predicate agreeing with the items.
segments.s245-g293: چو here = ‘when’; از بر گاه خسرو = a classical phrasing ‘from (از) the presence/court (بر گاه) of Khosrow’ (گاه = occasion/place, بر گاه = court/presence). برفت is an archaic past form meaning ‘he went away/left’ (past of رفتن with the prefix ب-). The sequence gives the temporal clause ‘when Giv left the king’s court’.
segments.s245-g294: ز هر سو = ‘from every side/direction’ (ز = from, هر سو = every side). سواران is the direct object ‘horsemen/riders’ and فرستاد is the past tense ‘he sent / dispatched’. The final token تفت is problematic in this list (its provided senses do not match the context) and therefore treated as uncertain or corrupt here; the core meaning is ‘he dispatched horsemen in all directions’.
segments.s245-g295: بجستن is an infinitival/verbal noun ‘to seek / searching’ and with گرفتند (‘they took/they began’) forms a classical periphrasis meaning ‘they set about searching’. گرد جهان = ‘around/about the world’ (گرد = around, جهان = world). Together: ‘they set about searching the world’.
segments.s245-g296: که introduces the purpose/result clause: یابد here is a subjunctive/optative sense ‘may/ might find’ (third person). مگر functions as ‘if / perhaps / except’ in this classical context (introducing the possibility clause). زو = from him (classical contraction of از او). بجایی نشان is a noun phrase ‘a fitting/appropriate sign/trace’ (بجایی modifying نشان). The clause means ‘to see whether they might find any fitting sign of him’.
segments.s245-g297: Word order is noun phrase first: 'همه شهر ارمان و تورانیان' = 'the whole city, Arman, and the Turanians.' 'همه' (modifier) precedes the noun 'شهر' to mean 'entire/whole.' 'ارمان' functions here as a proper name or apposition to 'شهر' (not one of the listed senses), and the coordinating conjunction 'و' links that apposition to 'تورانیان.'
segments.s245-g298: 'سپردند' is a past tense verb (3rd person plural) meaning 'they handed over/entrusted.' The conjunction 'و' links the two clauses. 'نامد' here functions negatively/elliptically (poetic form) meaning 'did not come / there came no' (not the listed nominal senses), followed by the preposition-like poetic 'ز' = 'from' and the agent 'بیژن.' 'نشان' is the object meaning 'sign/trace/token' and completes the clause 'نامد ز بیژن نشان' = 'no sign came from Bijan.'
segments.s245-g299: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' The compound noun phrase 'نوروز فرخ' places the epithet 'فرخ' (auspicious/fortunate) after the festival name. 'فراز' here is adverbial 'on high/aloft,' and 'آمدش' is the past-tense verb with the enclitic -ش 'to him' (came to him). Word order is typical Persian: temporal conjunction + subject phrase + adverb + verb(+enclitic).
segments.s245-g300: This line begins with the shortened/prepositional 'ب' (= 'to/into'), followed by 'دان' which here is not functioning as the listed verb 'to know' (the line reads as a fixed poetic sequence 'بدان' = 'to that'); the following noun 'جام' (cup) is modified by the adjective 'روشن' (bright), and 'نیاز آمدش' is a verb phrase meaning 'a supplication/need came to him' where 'آمدش' again carries the enclitic -ش 'to him.' The object 'نیاز' (supplication) is the thing that 'arrives.'
segments.s245-g301: 'بیامد' is a past-tense verb 'came' (here 'came upon/into'); 'دل پهلوان' is the noun phrase 'the hero's heart' with 'دل' ('heart') preceding the head noun 'پهلوان' for poetic emphasis; 'پر' is a prepositive adjective meaning 'full (of)' and modifies 'امید' (hope). So the phrase reads literally 'came the hero's heart full of hope.'
segments.s245-g302: The preposition 'ز' + 'بهر' together form 'for/on account of/for the sake of.' 'پسر' is the beneficiary/target 'son.' 'گوژ' is an adjectival epithet 'humpbacked' modifying the son, and 'گشته' is a perfect/passive participle meaning 'has become/turned,' here 'گوژ گشته' = 'having become hunchbacked.' 'نوان' follows as a descriptive name or epithet (listed as 'youthful' but in context functions as a proper name/epithet). Word order: purpose phrase then the described person.
segments.s245-g303: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. 'خسرو' is the subject (here the king's name). 'رخ گیو' places 'رخ' (face) before the name 'گیو' in an appositive/genitive relation: 'Giv's face.' 'پژمرده' is a predicate adjective 'withered/pale/dejected' describing that face, and 'دید' is the past-tense verb 'saw.' Typical SOV ordering is present: subject (implicit/expressed) + object phrase + predicate + verb.
segments.s245-g304: The phrase 'دلش را' is the object with the possessive enclitic '-ش' (his) and the object marker 'را' emphasizing the definite object. 'بدرد' here means 'to cause pain' (i.e., 'was torn/hurt'); 'اندر' = 'in/within' locates the pain internally; 'آزرده' is an adjective 'distressed/troubled.' The final 'دید' repeats the perception verb 'he saw,' so the clause means 'he saw his heart torn and inwardly distressed.'
segments.s245-g73: گر is the conditional particle 'if' introducing the clause. از من is a prepositional phrase 'from me' (object of separation), جدا is an adverb 'apart/separated', and ماند (third-person singular) means 'remains / is left'. فرزند من ('my child') is the subject/agent but appears after the verb here — classical Persian permits this inverted order for emphasis.
segments.s245-g74: روا دارم is a fixed evaluative phrase, literally 'I have [it] permissible' = 'I deem it allowable / fitting'; دارم is present first-person 'I have' used here as a modal/evaluative verb. ار is another conditional 'if / supposing', and بگسلد is the classical subjunctive/optative form (prefix ب- + گسلد) = 'he should break / breaks'. بند من 'my bond/fetter' is the direct object of بگسلد.
segments.s245-g75: روانم is 'my soul/spirit' (nominal). بدان here is the prepositional combination 'به + آن' = 'to that' (often written together in poetry), introducing the destination; جای نیکان = 'the place of the good/righteous'. بری is an adjectival/participial notion 'free (from)' modifying روانم or indicating release — the verb of motion ('to go') is omitted (elliptical) in the poetic line, so the phrase reads as a wish: 'may my soul, freed, go to that place of the righteous.'
segments.s245-g76: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'; درد دل من = 'the pain / sorrow of my heart' (درد modifying دل with من as possessive). تو آگه‌تری is comparative: آگه‌تر + -ی (second-person suffix) = 'you are more aware / you know better' — the speaker addresses the addressee as better informed about his own heartache.
segments.s245-g77: مرا here (written as م + را) is the object/topic 'to me / for me' (را is the definite/object marker). خود ... بود is emphatic: 'he himself was'. ز گیتی هم places the worldly scope ('from/for the world too' → 'the whole world'), and بس 'and enough' closes the clause: 'for me, he himself was the world and that sufficed.' The verb بود is the past/be-form used in the simple past sense.
segments.s245-g78: The line begins with the exclamative چه 'what a...', repeated for emphasis. انده گسار is an older/poetic compound meaning a disposer or reliever of sorrow (a lexeme here used poetically), و چه فریادرس 'and what a rescuer in cries' (فریادرس = one who comes to aid when one cries out). The structure is parallel exclamation: 'what X and what Y'.
segments.s245-g79: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. بخت بد 'bad fortune' (bakhte bad) is the subject phrase, and کردش is 'made him / caused him' — کرد + object suffix -ش = 'did to him / caused him'. از من جدا indicates separation 'away from me'. The verb کرد makes the clause active: 'now bad fortune has made him separate from me.'
segments.s245-g80: بماندم is first-person past/stative 'I remained / I was left' (ب- prefix with ماندم). چنین 'thus/so' modifies the following predicate, در جهان is the locative 'in the world', and مبتلا is predicate adjective 'afflicted / stricken', giving 'I remain thus afflicted in the world.'
segments.s245-g65: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. گفتار گرگینش is a noun phrase 'Gorgin's speech/words' with the enclitic -ش marking 'his' (Gorgin). آمد here is a simple past 'came/reached' and بگوش ('to the ear') completes the verb phrase: literally 'when Gorgin's words came to (his) ear' = 'when he heard Gorgin.'
segments.s245-g66: ز اسب اندر افتاد is a compact past clause: ز (from) + اسب (horse) + اندر افتاد (fell inward) = 'fell off/from the horse.' The following و زو رفت هوش is coordinate: و 'and' + (ز) 'from' + او contracted here as 'زو' + رفت هوش 'went (his) awareness' = 'and from him went his consciousness' (i.e. he fainted). رفت is used in the sense 'depart/vanish' for awareness.
segments.s245-g67: بخاک اندرون شد is a verbal phrase: بخاک 'into the earth/ground' + اندرون شد 'became inside' = 'sank/buried into the ground.' سرش uses the noun سر 'head' with the enclitic -ش 'his', and ناپدید is a predicate adjective 'vanished/unseen.' Word order is typical poetic inversion: locative phrase + شد + subject + predicate.
segments.s245-g68: همه جامه پهلوی بردرید is a short verbless/predicate clause where همه جامه 'all the garments' is the object/affected thing, پهلوی 'at the sides/flanks' specifies where, and بردرید (past) 'tore apart' states the action (implicitly by attackers). The order stresses the garments and their tearing at the sides.
segments.s245-g69: همی کند is the narrative imperfective 'kept doing' (همی as progressive/continuous marker + کند 'does'), followed by the object phrase موی از سر و ریش 'hair from the head and beard.' پاک here functions adverbially 'clean/entirely' (i.e. pulling the hair cleanly/utterly). The clause means 'he kept pulling out the hair from his head and beard.'
segments.s245-g70: خروشان modifies the following action as 'with tumult/roaring.' بسر here stands for the locative 'بر سر' ('on the head') in fused poetic form (none of the supplied senses fit exactly), and بر همی ریخت خاک is 'he cast/poured dust' repeatedly (همی + ریخت 'kept pouring'). Overall: 'tumultuously he kept throwing dust on his head.'
segments.s245-g71: همی گفت is a narrative particle + past verb: همی here gives a continuous/repetitive or emphatic flavour to گفت ('he said/kept saying'). کای is a vocative marker 'O', followed by کردگار سپهر a classical epithet 'Creator of the heavens' (noun + modifier). Word order is typical Persian: verb phrase همی گفت then the vocative phrase addressing God.
segments.s245-g72: تو گستردی is a 2nd-person past verb ('you spread/you have spread'). اندر دلم is a prepositional phrase 'within my heart' where دل 'heart' is followed by the enclitic possessive (here segmented as م) meaning 'my'; هوش و مهر are two coordinated nouns 'intellect/awareness and love/affection' serving as the object of گستردی. The conjunction و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s245-g265: ز بیژن = a prepositional phrase 'about/from Bijan' (ز is a poetic variant of از). مگر here functions as 'unless' rather than its other uses. آگهی is the noun 'news/information' placed before the verb (object before verb is common in Persian). یابما is an archaic jussive/subjunctive 2nd-person form 'may you find / you should find' (object آگهی precedes it).
segments.s245-g266: بدین کار = بدین is the compound be + این, 'in/with this' and کار 'matter/affair' — 'in this matter'. هشیار is an adjectival/imperative exhortation 'be alert'. بشتابما is an archaic optative/jussive ('ب + شتاب + ما') meaning roughly 'let us / may we hasten' or more loosely an urgent exhortation to hurry; the verb here links as an imperative-like close to هشیار.
segments.s245-g267: وگر introduces a protasis 'if'. دیر 'late' modifies the clause. یابیم is an archaic subjunctive/jussive form (here 1st plural) 'we should/ may find / we learn'. ز + و (written together as زو in classical texts) = 'from him/of him' (source of the news). آگهی is the noun 'news/information' governed by that preposition. Word order is topical: condition → time/adverb → verb phrase.
segments.s245-g268: تو is the addressee 'you' (topic). جای خرد 'the place/seat of wisdom' is a genitival phrase where جای (place/seat) is specified by خرد (wisdom). را is the definite-object marker (highlighting/definiteness). مگردان is the negative imperative 'do not turn/leave' (م- negative + گردان from گرداندن/گردیدن), and تهی is the predicate adjective 'empty'. The whole is an admonition not to abandon one's seat of reason.
segments.s245-g269: بمان is an imperative/hortative 'remain/stay'. تا ... بیاید is a temporal subordinate clause 'until ... comes' with بیاید as a subjunctive/optative 3rd-person form 'may come / comes'. مه here is the celestial 'moon' (classical usage) and فرودین is the month or proper name; together 'مه فرودین' = 'the moon (or month) of Farvardin'.
segments.s245-g270: که introduces the subordinate clause giving what will happen 'when/that'. بفروزد is a 3rd-person optative/indicative from فروزیدن 'to shine/illuminate' (here 'may/shall make shine'). اندر جهان = 'in the world'. هور دین is a genitival phrase 'the splendor/light (هور) of religion (دین)', i.e. 'religious splendor' — Persian allows the noun-noun sequence where the first noun modifies the second or vice versa by context (here 'splendor of religion').
segments.s245-g271: بدانگه is an adverb 'thereupon/then'. که introduces the subordinate/time clause 'when'. بر گل 'upon a flower' is the prepositional phrase (بر + گل). نشاندت is a past/jussive form with the 2nd-person enclitic object '-ت' meaning 'he set/placed you' (نشاند + ت). باد at the end is the optative particle 'may' (a conventional wish: 'may he ...').
segments.s245-g272: چو = 'when/as'. بر سر = 'upon (your) head' (بر + سر). همی is an archaic aspectual particle often used before verbs to mark continuing or immediate action (here it accompanies the verb فشاندت). گل is the object 'flower(s)'. فشاندت is a transitive verb with the 2nd-person enclitic '-ت' and here means 'he scatters/throws to you' (i.e. sprinkles flowers upon you). باد again is the optative 'may'.
segments.s245-g353: This is a relative clause introduced by که (here = 'who/that'). دارد is the present-tense verb 'has/is possessing' used statively ('is loyal'). بدین کار is a prepositional/ adverbial phrase meaning 'in/for this matter' (ب = 'to/for' + دین as part of the poetic form), and ما را وفا means 'fidelity/loyalty to us' where را marks ما as the definite object/beneficiary.
segments.s245-g354: که again links as 'who/that'. آرد is the (classical) 3rd-person singular of آوردن 'bring' (here 'would bring' / 'brings'). ز سختی is the prepositional phrase 'from/out of hardship' (ز is the poetic form of از). مر is an archaic/object pronoun meaning 'm(e)' or 'for me' placed before the rest of the object phrase, and او را رها is 'release/free him' with را marking او as the object; overall: 'who brings him release from hardship for me.'
segments.s245-g355: نشاید is an impersonal negative predicate meaning 'it is not fitting/it should not be.' جز introduces an exception: 'except.' از رستم indicates the agent/source 'from Rostam' (i.e. only Rostam can do it). تیز modifies چنگ ('sharp/keen' modifying 'claw' or 'grasp'), so تیز چنگ is a descriptive compound 'keen/sharp-clawed (hand).'
segments.s245-g356: که is the relative 'who/that' connecting to the previous clause. از ژرف دریا is the source phrase 'from the deep sea' (ژرف = 'deep', دریا = 'sea'). برآرد is the classical 3rd-person singular of برآوردن 'bring up/drag up' (transitive), and نهنگ is its object 'whale.'
segments.s245-g357: کمر بند here functions as an exhortation 'gird (your) waist' (an imperative phrasing common in classical Persian). و links to the next verb. برکش is an imperative/archaic verb meaning 'raise/set out' or 'pull up' and سوی نیمروز is the directional phrase 'toward noon/south' (سوی = 'toward', نیمروز = 'noon' / direction).
segments.s245-g358: شب is an adverbial/time noun 'at night' (or 'night'). از رفتن is a verbal-noun construction 'from/of going' (رفتن as a noun-like infinitive), راه is 'the road/way' and مأسا is a predicate noun 'misfortune/tragedy' with the copula omitted (a typical classical poetic verbless clause). و روز 'and day' connects the parallel time phrase.
segments.s245-g359: ببر is the 2nd-person singular imperative of بردن 'take!' directed to the messenger. نامه من is a possessive noun phrase 'my letter' (noun + pronoun), and بر رستما is the prepositional direction 'to Rostam' (بر = 'to/toward' here).
segments.s245-g360: مزن is a prohibitive/negative imperative here meaning 'do not (say/tell)' — in Persian 'داستان زدن' can mean 'to tell a tale', so مزن داستان را = 'do not tell the story.' بره is the subject 'a lamb' and بردما is a past-tense compound 'took us/carried us' (برد + ما) — a short clause 'a lamb took us' follows as a blunt statement; word order places the object pronoun inside the past verb form.
segments.s245-g217: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. گرگین is the subject (the proper name Gorgin) placed before the locative phrase بدرگاهِ خسرو ('to/at Khosrow's court'). رسید is a simple past, 3rd-person-singular verb 'arrived/reached', completing the clause.
segments.s245-g218: This is the main clause: the (omitted) subject 'he' (referring back to Gorgin) plus دید 'saw'. ز introduces accompaniment/association ('with/by' or 'among') here relating the troops to what is seen. گردان = 'troop(s)'. در is the noun 'door/gate' and شاه qualifies it ('the king's gate'). پردخته is a past-participial/adjectival form meaning 'arrayed/ornamented/well-composed'. The whole phrase describes what Gorgin saw: the king's gate adorned/attended by the troops.
segments.s245-g219: ز تیمارِ بیژن is a causal phrase: ز here means 'out of/because of' (motivation) and تیمارِ بیژن = 'care/concern for Bijan'. همه مهتران is the subject phrase 'all the chiefs/elders' (مهتران = leaders).
segments.s245-g220: ز درگاه = 'from the court' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). با گیو = 'with Giv' marks accompaniment. رفته is the past participle / past-tense verb 'gone/left' describing the action of the subject (همه مهتران). سران ('leaders/heads') reiterates/identifies the subject as chiefs; here it functions as a plural noun bound to رفته.
segments.s245-g221: همه 'all/everyone' is the topic. پر means 'full (of)' and takes ز + noun to indicate what they are filled with: ز درد = 'with pain/sorrow' (ز = 'from/of' giving cause). The coordinating و links the parallel phrase همه پر زرنج, repeating the structure. (Note: زرنج here is given as a place-name in the lexical options; grammatically it functions as the noun that follows پر.)
segments.s245-g222: This is a simile: همه 'all' + همچو 'like/as' + گم‌کرده 'lost/bereft' (participle used as noun/adjective) + صد گونه گنج 'a hundred kinds of treasure' (گونه = 'kind/type', گنج = 'treasure'). The structure compares the people's state to someone who has lost many treasures.
segments.s245-g223: پراگنده رای و پراگنده دل is a parallel noun phrase: پراگنده (= 'scattered') modifies رای ('counsel/plan') and دل ('heart') respectively. The adjective precedes the noun (common in Persian poetic word order), and و coordinates the two parallel descriptions.
segments.s245-g224: This clause describes the result: همه 'all' is the subject; خاکِ ره = 'the dust of the road' (خاک = 'dust', ره = 'way/road'). زِ اشک کرده combines ز ('by/with' here indicating instrument = 'with their tears') + اشک ('tears') + کرده ('made/done') so 'made (with) tears'. چو گل is a simile 'like a flower' — together: 'they had, with their tears, made all the road's dust like a flower.'
segments.s245-g241: ندانست is the past-tense negative verb (he did not know) with an implicit third-person subject. پاسخ چه گوید is an indirect question: چه (what) + گوید (present/subjunctive form used in classical Persian for 'to say/utter') meaning 'what (he) should say' or 'what to say'. بدوی is an archaic dative/benefactive form (equivalent to به وی / بدو) meaning 'to him' — it attaches as the indirect object of 'answer'.
segments.s245-g242: فروماند is a past intransitive verb meaning 'remained/was left' or 'fell back/was struck dumb'; بر جای is a prepositional phrase 'in/at his place' (بر = on/at + جای = place). بر زرد روی attaches as a descriptive phrase 'with a yellow/pale face' (زرد روی = pale-faced, adjective after noun), where the second بر functions like 'on/with'.
segments.s245-g243: زبان پر ز یافه uses the pattern Noun + پر + ز + Noun to mean 'tongue/speech full of X' (پر 'full (of)', ز = 'of/from'). Here زبان = 'speech/utterance' and یافه is the noun describing what it's full of. روان پر گناه repeats the same formula for the inner self: روان = 'soul/spirit', پر = 'full (of)', گناه = 'sin'. The noun phrases have an implicit possessive ('his').
segments.s245-g244: رخان زرد و لرزان تن is a descriptive cluster: رخان (a facial/cheek-related word here) followed by adjectives زرد و لرزان modifying the person, and تن = 'body'. از بیم شاه is a causal prepositional phrase 'from/because of fear of the king' (از = from/because of, بیم = fear, شاه = king). The whole segment gives the physical effect (paleness, trembling) and its cause.
segments.s245-g245: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). گفتارها یک بدیگر نماند is an inverted order typical of poetry: گفتارها = 'the words/conversation(s)', یک (here) = 'one' / 'any' or emphasizing 'not even one', بدیگر = 'to one another' (i.e. between them), and نماند is past negative 'was not left / remained not' → 'there remained no words between them'.
segments.s245-g246: برآشفت is a past verb meaning 'he grew angry/was agitated' (بر- prefix + آشفته). وز here coordinates the clause that follows ('and/thereupon' in classical usage). پیش تختش = 'before his throne' (پیش = in the presence of/before, تختش = his throne). براند is the past form meaning 'he drove away' (بر- + راند), so the clause says he drove the person away from before his throne. The object (him) is understood from context but not explicit in this line.
segments.s245-g247: همش خیره‌سر دید هم بدگمان: همش 'entirely/wholly' modifies خیره‌سر 'headstrong/stubborn'; دید is the past 'he saw/regarded' (used idiomatically as 'he considered him'); the second هم before بدگمان means 'also' or 'even', so the whole phrase says 'he regarded him as wholly headstrong and also suspicious.'
segments.s245-g248: بدشنام is an epithet 'ill-reputed / infamous' modifying the subject. بگشاد is the past form 'opened' (بـ- prefix + گشاد) and زبان 'tongue' (here idiomatically 'mouth / speech'); the word order places the epithet before the verb and the subject name (خسرو) appears in the line as the actor who 'opened his tongue' (i.e. began to speak).
segments.s245-g249: بدو is a prepositional/dative-like clitic meaning 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense 'said' (here reported speech) — the provided senses list doesn't include the verbal sense so it is flagged below; نشنیدی is the 2nd-person singular perfect negative of شنیدن, meaning 'have you not heard'; آن داستان is a determiner + noun 'that story/tale' serving as the direct object of نشنیدی.
segments.s245-g250: که introduces a subordinate relative/clause-of-content 'that/which'; دستان here functions as the subject/nominal 'the tales' (poetic plural of داستان) or a reference to the tales being spoken of; زدست is a poetic prepositional/agentive expression 'by the hand of' (literally), linking the action of handing/telling; از گه باستان is a prepositional time-phrase 'from the time of old' (از + گه 'occasion/time' + باستان 'ancient'), giving the temporal source.
segments.s245-g251: که‌گر is an archaic fused conditional particle meaning 'if'; شیر is the subject 'lion' (often a metaphor for a brave man); با + کین marks accompaniment/attitude — 'with vengeance/hatred' — and گودرزیان is the object of that hatred (the Gudarzis). Word order is Subject + prepositional phrase describing manner/feeling.
segments.s245-g252: بسیچد is an archaic/poetic verb form (the exact root is irregular here) in the conditional clause describing the lion's action; تنش را is 'his body' with the possessive clitic ش and the definite object marker را (object of the verb); سر آید زمان literally 'the time comes for the head' — آید is the verb 'come' and زمان 'time' — this idiomatic inversion means 'the time will come for his head' (i.e. his head will be taken).
segments.s245-g253: اگر marks a conditional 'if'; نیستی is 2nd-person singular negative ('you are not' / 'you do not...'); از پی is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in pursuit of' or 'after' and takes نام بد 'bad name/reputation' as its object — together 'if you are not after a bad reputation.'
segments.s245-g254: وگر is the contrasting conditional 'but if' or 'and if'; پیش یزدان is 'in the presence of / before God' (پیش = in front of/in the presence of, یزدان = God/divine); سرانجام بد is a verbless result phrase 'the outcome/end will be bad' — سرانجام 'end/outcome' plus adjective بد 'bad'. The clause warns that if one seeks bad fame, the divine judgment/end will be unfavorable.
segments.s245-g255: بفرمودمی is a first‑person past/honorific form 'I commanded / I said' (classical/formal); تا introduces a purpose or result clause 'so that / in order that' which governs the following verb; سرت را ز تن is the object phrase 'your head (سرت) — را marks it as definite — from (ز) the body (تن)', i.e. 'your head from your body.'
segments.s245-g256: بکندی is an archaic/optative or past-subjunctive verbal form meaning roughly 'should cut / would cut' and is the verb of the purpose clause after تا; بکردار here is best read as بِـچونِ/به‌کردار (by the deed/manner of) in poetic usage — the provided senses ('virtuous') don't fit the context so that sense is flagged below; مرغ اهرمن is the agent phrase 'the bird of Ahriman' (مرغ 'bird', اهرمن = Ahriman/the devil) indicating the violent instrument/agent in the simile or command.
segments.s245-g233: This is an optative clause introduced by بادا (a poetic 'may'). Word order places the object phrase سر دشمنان تو (the heads of your enemies) before the verb phrase بادا بگاز ('may ... bite'). بگاز is the subjunctive/optative form of گازیدن 'to bite' and here takes the earlier noun phrase as its patient/theme.
segments.s245-g234: بریده is a past-participial adjective 'cut off' modifying an understood head; چنان کان is an archaic comparative phrase 'so/as if', with کان here meaning 'as if'. سران گراز is a noun phrase 'the heads of boars' (سران = heads, plural), following Persian adjective/noun patterns in poetry.
segments.s245-g235: بدندانها is an adverbial/phrase 'with (his) teeth' (i.e. showing or baring the teeth); چون before the verb here functions temporally/illustratively 'in that way' or 'when'; نگه کرد is a compound verb: نگه (a look, a gaze) + کرد (past 'made/did'), meaning 'gave a look' or 'looked'; شاه follows, the subject placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s245-g236: بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'he asked'; و links the two actions; گفتش is 'he said to him' (گفت + enclitic ش = 'to him'); که introduces the content clause; چون بستگی به پرسشی است here as interrogative 'how'; بود is past 'was' and راه is the question's noun 'the road/way' — together 'that how was the way'.
segments.s245-g237: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; ماند is past 'remained/was left'; از تو جدا is an adverbial phrase 'separated from you' (از + pronoun + جدا = apart); بیژنا here is a proper name (Bijan) in the clause, placed at the end in poetic order.
segments.s245-g238: بروبر is an adverb 'right in front / face-to-face'; چه here is exclamatory ('what' / 'how' in an exclamation); بد is 'evil/bad' modifying ساخت 'made/did' (past of ساختن, here 'devised' or 'contrived'); آهرمنا is the proper name Ahriman (the Evil One) as the subject of ساخت.
segments.s245-g239: چو is a conjunction 'when'; خسرو is the proper name 'Khosrow' (the king); چنین 'thus/so' modifies گفت (here 'said so'); گرگین (Gorgin) is a personal name placed after the verb in poetic order; بجای is an adverbial 'in his place / remaining where he was'.
segments.s245-g240: فرو ماند is a compound meaning 'fell/was left down' or 'remained down/abashed' (فرو = down, ماند = remained). خیره is an adjective/adverb 'staring / astonished' describing the state; همیدون is a proper name (here the person who is in that state); بپای is a verbal/adverbial phrase meaning 'to remain/stand (in place)' — together describing someone stunned and staying rooted there.
segments.s245-g321: همه بودنیها is a plural noun phrase 'all beings' (همه before the noun is a universal quantifier; بودنیها is the plural of بودنی). بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him/unto him' placed after the noun phrase (poetic word order). اندرا is an appositive/name (Indra) identifying that 'him' — a common poetic placement of a proper name after a pronoun.
segments.s245-g322: بدیدی is a past tense (3rd person singular) verb 'he saw/he beheld' (classical/past narrative). جهاندار افسونگرا is the noun phrase functioning as the object or complement: جهاندار 'world‑ruler/sovereign' followed by the adjectival epithet افسونگرا 'sorcerous, magic‑using' (modifier follows the noun in poetry). The subject is supplied from the preceding context (Andra).
segments.s245-g323: نگه کرد is a compound verbal expression: نگه (a noun 'gaze/watch') plus the light verb کرد 'did' to mean 'he looked/kept watch'. و پس links actions ('and then'). جام بنهاد is a past tense verbal clause: بنهاد (past of نهادن) 'he placed/put' with جام as its object. پیش is adverbial 'in front, before' (i.e. placed the cup before someone).
segments.s245-g324: بدید is again past 3rd‑singular 'he saw' with the subject carried over from the previous clause. اندرو here is the locative/inside reading 'in it / within him' (poetic form meaning 'within it' or 'within him') and precedes بودنیها 'the beings', so اندرو بودنیها = 'the beings within it/him'. ز بیش is an adverbial phrase 'from excess' = 'in great abundance' (ز + بیش used idiomatically to indicate abundance).
segments.s245-g325: بهر هفت کشور is a directional/limit phrase: بهر 'toward/throughout' + هفت کشور 'the seven countries/lands'. همی بنگرید is an archaic/poetic present/habitual form (بـ + نگرید) meaning 'he looked/gazed' (همی adds the ongoing/habitual sense 'still/continually'). Word order places the directional phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s245-g326: ز بیژن is 'of/from Bijan' (ز used poetically to mark source/possession: 'a trace of Bijan'). بجایی functions adverbially here 'in any place/anywhere' (poetic use of بجا), نشانی 'a sign/trace' is the direct object, and ندید is past negative 'he did not see' (lit. 'saw not'). The whole clause = 'he saw no sign of Bijan anywhere.'
segments.s245-g327: سوی is a preposition 'toward/to'; کشور گرگساران is a genitive/compound phrase 'the country/land of the wolf‑people' (گرگساران = 'wolves' or 'wolfish folk' used as a collective). رسید is past 3rd‑singular 'arrived/reached'. The noun phrase precedes the verb in typical epic word order.
segments.s245-g328: بفرمان یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command of God' (بـِ + فرمان). مر is the dative clitic 'to me' (archaic form), او را is the accusative 'him' with the object marker را, and بدید here is used transitively as 'caused to be seen / showed' (past 3rd singular). Put together: 'By God's command he showed him to me.'
segments.s245-g273: Word order is poetic: subject زمین (the earth) begins the phrase, followed by the noun phrase چادر سبز (lit. 'tent green' — noun then adjective, the normal Persian order). در is the preposition 'in/with' and پوشدا is an archaic past/passive form of پوشیدن meaning 'was covered' or 'was clad' (the -دا ending is an older past-participle/preterite marker frequently used in Ferdowsi). Together: 'the earth was clad/in a green tent'.
segments.s245-g274: هوا (the air) is the subject; بر گلان is a prepositional phrase 'upon/over the flowers' (بر + plural گل). زار here functions adverbially 'lamenting/with lament' (a poetic modifier). بخروشدا is an older past/preterite form of خروشیدن with the prefix ب- and the -دا ending, meaning 'roared/was in a roar' (poetic past). The whole line: 'the air, lamenting, roared over the flowers.'
segments.s245-g275: بهرسو is an adverbial/prepositional phrase meaning 'to every side/quarter' (literally 'toward each direction'); شود is the copula/auxiliary 'becomes' or optative 'may be' (a poetic use giving a sense of wishing or establishing); پاک فرمان ما is a noun phrase 'our pure command/decree' with ما as post-nominal possessive. The clause thus reads as an optative/passive statement: 'may our pure decree be made known to every quarter.'
segments.s245-g276: پرستش is the noun 'worship' (the content of the decree). که introduces a relative/nominal clause 'which/that'; فرمود is a classical past verb 'said/commanded' placed before its subject in poetic inversion; یزدان ما is 'our God' (یزدان = God, ما = our). The fragment therefore means '"Worship," which our God commanded.'
segments.s245-g277: بخواهم is the first-person present/subjunctive of خواستن, here a wish 'I desire' or 'may I desire to'; من is the emphatic subject 'I' (post-verbal for emphasis); آن جام گیتی نمای is a noun phrase where جام 'cup' is modified postpositively by گیتی نمای ('world-revealing' or 'that shows the world') — نمای is the verbal/adjectival element meaning 'showing'. The whole unit: 'I desire that cup which shows the world.'
segments.s245-g278: شوم is the first-person subjunctive/optative of شدن 'may I become/appear'; پیش is the preposition 'before/in the presence of'; یزدان is 'God'. The pair بباشم بپای are two verbs with the augmenting poetic prefix ب-: بباشم (from بودن) 'may I be/remain' and بپای (from پاییدن/پاییدن) 'abide/stand firm' — together giving an optative sense 'may I appear before God and abide/remain steadfast'. The sequence shows repeated optative verbs for emphasis.
segments.s245-g279: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. هفت کشور is a quantified noun phrase 'the seven countries/realms'. بدو is an indirect-object clitic-like adverb 'to him / unto it' (here functioning as 'bestowed to him' or 'given to him'); اندرا is a proper name (Indra/Andra) placed after the clause in apposition or address — a typical poetic inversion. The line asks 'Where shall I see the seven countries given to him, Andra?'.
segments.s245-g280: ببینم is the first-person present/subjunctive 'I shall/will see' (here prospective). بر و بوم is a fixed, poetic pairing meaning 'roof and soil' = 'towns and lands/inhabited places'. هر کشورا is 'each country' (هر 'each' + poetic plural/collective کشورا). The word order places the verb before the prepositional/object phrase, a common poetic arrangement: 'I will see the towns and lands of every country.'
segments.s245-g57: This clause states the speaker's attitude toward their life. مرا (here split into م + را) functions as the 1st-person object/topic ('for me' / 'my'); جان شیرین is the noun phrase 'sweet life' (literal 'sweet soul') with شیرین as an epithet after جان. نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'ought not / should not', and همی is an archaic/poetic particle used here for emphasis or continuity (a classical survivant). Word order is topic/object (مرا) + subject noun phrase (جان شیرین) + modal (نباید) + particle (همی).
segments.s245-g58: This conditional-adjunct clause qualifies the previous line. کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. خوارتر‌گر is a fused conditional 'if (it becomes) more lowly/abase(d)', with -گر marking the 'if/when' sense. برآید is a present/subjunctive-type verb 'comes about / should arise' (classical subjunctive/optative flavor), and the final همی again is the archaic particle reinforcing the verb. Together the segment reads 'if it should now become even more lowly'.
segments.s245-g59: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. چشمم is 'my eye' (چشم + possessive suffix -م), with the suffix here listed separately; بروی (classical orthography of برِ روی / بر روی) means 'upon/onto (your face)' and is followed by تو 'you/your (face)'. آید is a present-tense verb 'comes' used with چو to mean 'when (my eye) comes upon/looks on'. ز + شرم means 'because of/from shame' (ز as causal 'from/because of'). Word order: temporal particle (چو) + posses-suffixed noun (چشمم) + locative expression (بروی تو) + verb (آید) + causal phrase (ز شرم).
segments.s245-g60: This is the main clause: بپالایم is a 1st-person present/jussive form meaning 'I purify/cleanse' (classical preverb ب + verb پالاییدن). از دیدگان literally 'from the eyes' marks source (i.e. from my eyes). آب گرم is 'warm water', here a metaphor for tears, with گرم qualifying آب. The sequence implies 'I will cleanse/wipe (them) — warm water from my eyes'.
segments.s245-g61: کنون 'now' frames the clause. هیچ has the negative sense 'no/none' and modifies مندیش (an older/poetic noun meaning concern/thought or 'one who cares'). کو را is an interrogative/relative-type element here used archaically to mean 'who/which person' (treated as object/topic and marked by را). بجان is an oath-like or evaluative phrase 'by the life (of)' or 'to life', functioning idiomatically to intensify the following claim. Overall: 'Now there is no one (کو را) whom, by life, ...' (leading into the next clause).
segments.s245-g62: نیامد گزند is a fixed negation + noun phrase meaning 'no harm came' / 'no harm befell'. The coordinating و links to بگویم نشان: بگویم here is the 1st-person present/subjunctive stem 'that I say / I will say' (classical form), and نشان is 'sign / token' (object). So the clause says 'no harm came, and I will give/say a sign.' Note: some listed senses for بگویم are nominal in the metadata; here it functions verbally.
segments.s245-g63: چو 'when' opens the temporal clause. اسب پسر is 'the youth's/son's horse' (noun + possessor). دید is the past tense 'he saw'. گرگین here is most plausibly the proper name 'Gorgin' (a character), and بدست is a classical phrase meaning 'by the hand of / in the possession of' (often 'in the hand of' or 'at the hands of'). Word order: temporal marker + noun phrase (اسب پسر) + verb (دید) + agent/adjunct (گرگین بدست).
segments.s245-g64: This describes the horse's condition: پر از خاک 'full of dust' (پر indicating 'full (of)'); و آسیمه 'and distraught/hurried' (آسیمه = distressed/distraught). برسان is an imperative/infinitival form 'bring/bring to' or 'deliver', and مست here is figurative 'intoxicated/dazed' (a common poetic epithet meaning stunned or excited). Together: 'full of dust and distraught — bring (him) — (for he is) dazed.'
segments.s245-g25: پس آنگه are sequential adverbs (literally 'then, then') placing the action in sequence; سوی شهر ایران is a prepositional phrase ('toward the city of Iran') with the preposition سوی before its object; شتافت is the past tense, 3rd-person singular of شتافتن, meaning 'he hastened/ran off'. Word order is classical Persian: adverb(s) + direction phrase + verb.
segments.s245-g26: شب و روز 'night and day' is a coordinated temporal phrase; آرام here functions as 'rest/peace' (i.e. 'peaceful rest') and, together with خوردن (the infinitive 'eating'), forms two coordinated objects of the verb نیافت; نیافت is an archaic past form 'did not find/obtain' (negative is built into نیافت). In classical Persian the infinitive (خوردن) is often used as the direct object of یافتن/نیافتن.
segments.s245-g27: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آگاهی 'news/information' is the subject that آمد 'came'; ز گرگین marks the source 'from Gorgin' (ز = from, classical poetic preposition) and بشاه 'to the king' is the indirect object (به‌شاه → 'to the king') placed after the source in this poetic word order.
segments.s245-g28: که introduces a reported-content clause 'that ...'; بیژن is the subject of that clause; نبودست is an archaic past form meaning 'was not' (classical negative past of بودن); با او براه literally 'with him on the road' (با = with, او = him, براه = en route/on the way). The clause therefore reports that Bijan was not accompanying him on the road.
segments.s245-g29: بگفت is the past-tense verb 'he said/told'; این سخن is the direct object 'these words/this message'; گیو را marks the addressee 'to Giv' (را here emphasizes the object), and شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the object in poetic order.
segments.s245-g30: بدان is a fused classical imperative/jussive (ب + دان) meaning 'know this' or 'take this to heart'; تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that'; ز گرگین 'from Gorgin' gives the source/target of the ensuing action; کند is a 3rd-person form of کردن used here in a subjunctive/optative sense 'may/might do' (common after تا to indicate purpose); خواستار is a noun 'petitioner/one who asks' — together کند خواستار conveys 'make/request' or more naturally here 'so that you may make a demand / ask of Gorgin'.
segments.s245-g31: پس is a sequential adverb 'then'; آگاهی آمد 'news came' is the main clause; همانگه is an adverb 'at that very moment / immediately'; بگیو here is the archaic fused form of به + گیو ('to Giv')—a classical cliticized prepositional phrase meaning 'to Giv' (note: this exact 'to-Giv' sense is not listed among the provided senses for this token).
segments.s245-g32: ز introduces the reported content 'about/regarding'; گم بودن is the nominalized infinitive 'the being-lost / that [he] was lost' (گم = lost, بودن = to be); رزمزن پور نیو is a name phrase / epithet: رزمزن 'warrior' (epithet), پور 'son (of)' as a patronymic element, and نیو is a proper name (here the name of the father or of the person, not the dictionary senses given), so the whole phrase means 'the warrior, son of Niu' or 'the warrior's son Niu' depending on context.
segments.s245-g161: بگرگین یکی is an adjective (بگرگین, 'wolf-like') followed by یکی 'one, a certain (person/creature)' as the subject; بانگ برزد is a verbal phrase where برزد is the archaic past (3rd sg.) of زدن used to mean 'utter/let out' here and بلند is a postpositive adjective/adverb meaning 'loud'. The clause order (adj + یکی + object/verb phrase) is typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s245-g162: که‌ای introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that, who'); بدکنش is a compound noun/adjective meaning 'evil-doer' and here functions as a nominal description; ریمن is a proper name (or place-name) and پرگزند is a predicate adjective meaning 'full of harm' or 'hurtful'. Classical Persian often omits a copula (بود), so the clause is verbless with the predicate adjective standing as comment on the antecedent.
