segments.s245-g163: تو بردی is a past 2nd-person singular clause ('you took' / 'you carried off'); ز من marks source ('from me'); the coordinated nouns شید و ماه are the things taken. مرا here (written as م + را in the tokenization) functions as the possessive/object clitic referring back to the speaker ('my' / 'me' in older usage), attached after the nouns — a classical placement for possessive/object marking.
segments.s245-g164: گزین here is best read as 'chosen/selected' (an adjectival/epithet) modifying the following nouns; سواران و شاه مرا is a coordinated noun phrase 'my chosen horsemen and my king' with مرا again marking possession/object. Word order places the epithet before the nouns and the possessive/clitic after them — a pattern frequent in poetry.
segments.s245-g165: فگندی is an obscure or dialectal lexical item (likely a name or rare word) and appears here as the agent/subject; مرا is the object/possessive clitic 'me/my' attached after it. در introduces a locative/setting phrase; تک probably means 'single' or 'single combat' and the reduplication پوی پوی is poetic emphasis of پوی 'seeking/striving' (repetition intensifies the idea 'continually seeking'). The phrase as a whole lists the difficult condition the speaker is placed into.
segments.s245-g166: بگرد is an imperative (or jussive) of گردیدن/گَردیدن meaning 'turn/search'; جهان اندرون is a locative compound 'the world within' or 'the inner world' (noun + اندرون 'inside'); چاره‌جوی is an agentive participle/noun 'seeker of remedy' placed after the locative as a vocative or descriptive address. The clause is imperative and addresses someone who should go search inwardly for a solution.
segments.s245-g167: پس اکنون 'so now' is a temporal/conclusive phrase; بدستان and بند and فریب list instruments/conditions ('evil hands', 'bonds/fetters', 'deceit'). In classical Persian such a series often functions adverbially or as the circumstances under which the question that follows must be considered.
segments.s245-g168: کجا یابی is a rhetorical question 'where will you find' (یابی is 2nd-person present/future of یافتن used in questions); آرام و خواب و شکیب are three coordinated nouns 'rest/peace, sleep, and patience' serving as the objects sought. The word-order places the interrogative حَدَف (کجا) first, the verb next, and the coordinated nouns after it — a normal classical pattern.
segments.s245-g345: ز here is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing the cause of the state; the clause follows the pattern Subject‑become‑Adjective with شده (perfect/participial) + ناامید meaning 'became hopeless'. The phrase پیوند و خویشان is the cause/objects of that hopelessness: 'hopeless of ties and of relatives.'
segments.s245-g346: گرازنده is used adjectivally/participially ('boar‑like') describing his manner; بر سان is a two‑word comparative/prepositional phrase (بر 'against/on' + سان 'like/as') and introduces the simile; یک‌شاخ modifies بید ('a one‑horned willow'), the noun of the simile. The whole phrase reads 'he (acted) like a boar against a lone one‑branched willow.'
segments.s245-g347: This is a verbless (poetic) descriptive clause where پر (here 'full (of)') functions as a predicative adjective: 'دو چشمش پر از خون' = 'both his eyes were full of blood.' The sequence دل پر ز درد parallels it ('his heart full of pain'), with ز/از marking the source/content ('of').
segments.s245-g348: زبانش ('his tongue' or 'his speech') is the topic; ز خویشان 'because of/from his relatives' gives the reason, and پر از یاد ('full of remembrance') is again a predicative phrase. کرد is a light past verb here ('made'/'became'), completing the predicate: 'his tongue, on account of his relatives, was filled with remembrance/mentioning.'
segments.s245-g349: چو introduces a temporal/simile clause here and should be read 'when'; ابر بهاران 'the spring cloud' is the subject of the following verbal noun; ببارندگی is a gerund/verbal noun 'raining' or 'during rain,' so the whole clause = 'when the spring cloud rains.'
segments.s245-g350: همی is a habitual/continuative adverb ('constantly, ever'); مرگ جوید is a simple finite verb phrase where جوید ('seeks') here carries the sense of longing/desiring, so 'he longs for death.' بدَان زندگی is a compact prepositional phrase meaning 'in/for that life' (بدان is an archaic/poetic locative/comparative formation), giving the sphere in which he seeks death.
segments.s245-g351: بدین چاره اکنون is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/with this expedient now' (بدین = به + این). که introduces a subordinate clause ('when/if'), and جنبد ز جای is a present/stative verb phrase 'he moves/stirs from his place' (ز جای = 'from his place'). The sentence thus sets the temporal/conditional frame for what follows.
segments.s245-g352: که خیزد میان بسته این را بپای continues the conditional: خیزد 'he may rise' (present/subjunctive-like sense), میان بسته 'in the closed/packed midst' describes where he rises, این را is the object/topic 'this', and بپای is an imperative/jussive verb meaning 'be on guard / lie in wait / attend' (literally 'keep watch' or 'guard it'). The verb is placed at the end, completing the warning.
segments.s245-g113: برآمد is a past/perfective verb placed before its subject in poetic word order meaning 'arose/appeared'. یکی ('a certain') follows to introduce an indefinite subject, گور is the subject meaning the wild ass/onager, and زان ('from that') marks the origin (prepositional), followed by مرغزار ('meadow') as the source. The sequence is verb → indefinite subject → noun phrase of origin, typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s245-g114: کزان is a relative/prepositional compound ('from which' / 'of whom') referring back to the onager; خوبتر is a comparative adjective 'more handsome/more beautiful'. کس نبیند is a negative universal statement ('no one sees') where نبیند is negative present/indicative (in classical usage it functions like 'has not seen' / 'sees not'). نگار here means 'the beautiful one' / 'beloved' and is the thing being compared—literally: 'from whom (i.e. than whom) no one sees a better beauty.'
segments.s245-g115: This is a string of adjectival descriptions applied elliptically to the subject (the onager). بکردار ('of noble/beautiful bearing') and گلگون ('rosy, flower-colored') are adjectival epithets; the poetic order places the possessive name گودرز before موی (hair) for metrical effect, so گودرز موی reads as 'hair like Goudarz's' (i.e. reminiscent of that hero's hair). There is no copula; adjectives and genitive phrase modify the implied noun.
segments.s245-g116: چو introduces a simile ('like/as'). خنگ is an adjective 'dazed/overcome' modifying شباهنگ ('nightingale'); فرهاد is the possessor/associate (Farhad) and روی ('face') names the part being likened. Poetic word order places modifiers and names in sequence, so read as: 'its face like Farhad's nightingale, dazed' — a compact simile equating the animal's face with the beloved, enraptured nightingale of Farhad.
segments.s245-g117: چو introduces simile ('like'). سیمش is سیم (silver) with the possessive suffix -ش ('its silver/ silvery'), so 'سیمش دو پا' literally 'its two legs like silver'. و is the coordinating 'and'; چو پولاد سم = 'and hooves like steel' (پولاد = steel, سم = hoof). The passage uses a series of similes to describe limbs and their luster/hardness.
segments.s245-g118: Again چو is the comparative 'like'. شبرنگ is an adjective 'variegated / spotted' describing coloration; بیژن (Bijan) supplies the poetic comparison (read as 'like Bijan's' or 'as in Bijan'), and the following list سر و گوش و دم ('head and ear and tail') are the body parts being compared. Poetic inversion places the color and model before the nouns: 'head, ear and tail like Bijan's variegated (ones)'.
segments.s245-g119: بگردن here is 'with (a) neck' (بـ as instrumental/in combination with گردن), followed by چو شیر 'like a lion' — an adjectival simile for the neck. و connects to برفتن چو باد, where برفتن ('to go, to depart; here: its going/movement') is likened to باد ('the wind'). Together: 'with a lion-like neck and a motion like the wind.'
segments.s245-g120: تو is the vocative/second-person subject 'you'. گفتی is the past tense 'you said' (classical past of گفتن). که introduces the content clause ('that'); از marks origin/source ('from/of'); رخش is the name of a famous steed/warhorse (Rakhsh) or more generally 'his steed' here; دارد is present-tense 'has/possesses'; نژاد is 'lineage/breed'. The clause reports: 'you said that he has lineage from Rakhsh.'
segments.s245-g33: ز خانه ("from the house") is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb; بیامد is a past simple verb "came". In poetic inversion the patient/topic (دامان, "the hem/skirt") follows the verb rather than preceding it, and تا بکوی ("as far as the lane") marks the extent reached.
segments.s245-g34: دل is the noun/topic "heart" followed by an adjectival/circumstantial phrase از درد خسته ("weary from pain") where از introduces the cause. پر از آب روی is a compact predicative phrase: پر ("full (of)") governs an of-phrase از آب, with روی ("face") placed after water in poetic order to mean "the face (lit. with water)" — i.e. wet with tears.
segments.s245-g35: همی before a verb gives an iterative/continuous sense ("kept/was continually"). گفت here functions as a speech-verb (poetic past) followed by the reported clause بیژن نیامد ("Bijan did not come"). The final همی is emphatic/iterative again.
segments.s245-g36: بارمان is topicalized (the name Barman placed up front). ندانم is the first-person negative present/past idiom "I do not know/I knew not"; چه ماند is a verbless/elliptical question-clause "what remained/what became of (him)" and the sentence-ending همی again adds an emphatic/continuing tone.
segments.s245-g37: بفرمود is an honorific/past speech-verb "he commanded/said"; تا here introduces the content/purpose clause ("that / asking ..."). بور is an epithet/adjective "fair-haired" modifying کشواد (a personal name); را is the definite object/topic marker attaching to that name within the subordinate clause.
segments.s245-g38: کجا is the interrogative "where" placed initially; داشتی is second-person past/pret. form of داشتن ("you held/had"); روز فریاد را is the object "the day of outcry/lament" with را marking it as definite/accusative. The clause thus asks where the addressee had observed/held that day.
segments.s245-g39: بروبر is an adverb meaning "straight/on top/opposite"; نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past "they placed/put". زین خدنگ is the object phrase where زین ("this/that" or "saddle" in context) and خدنگ ("dagger") form a compound positional object meaning "that dagger (at/on the saddle)"; poetic word order places the adverb before the verb and the object after it.
segments.s245-g40: گرفته is a perfect/participial form "taken/seized" (here describing the dagger). بدل functions as "in return / as recompense" or adverbially "in revenge/thereupon"; گیو is the proper name Giv, and the phrase کین پلنگ literally places motive before agent: کین ("vengeance") + پلنگ ("the panther") → "the panther's act of vengeance". Together the participle and following nouns mean "(the dagger) taken by the panther in vengeance against Giv."
segments.s245-g121: بر بیژن آمد is a simple prepositional phrase + verb: بر (preposition, 'upon/against') + بیژن (proper name) + آمد (past of آمد‚ 'came'), so 'came upon Bijan'. چو introduces a simile ('like/as'), and پیلی نژند is the simile's noun + adjective: پیلی (elephantine) modified by نژند (pejorative 'base/wretched'). Word order places the simile after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s245-g122: افگند is the past verb 'threw/cast' with an omitted explicit subject (the beast). اندر is the locative preposition 'into/within', and کمند is the object 'lasso/snare'; the phrase orders adverbial/locative before the verb for rhythm: 'it cast (threw) a snare into/around (him)'. برو here functions adverbially/poetically to mark immediacy ('thereupon' / 'then proceeded to'), linking the action to the previous clause.
segments.s245-g123: فگندن and رفتن are infinitives/gerunds used nominally to mean 'the throwing' and 'the going/running'. همان بود ('was the same') uses همان ('the same') with بود (past of 'to be') to say the two actions matched. The coordinating و links the two clauses: 'the casting was the same and the running the same.'
segments.s245-g124: دوان is a verbal form meaning 'running/charging' (present participle/gerund sense) functioning as the action; it precedes its coordinated subjects 'گور و بیژن' (the onager and Bijan), a common poetic order. پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards', and اندر دمان is a locative phrase 'into/in the hem/skirt' (دِمان = edge or skirt of a hill/plain), giving the destination.
segments.s245-g125: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' marking cause; تازیدن is an infinitive/gerund here 'the charging/rushing' (used nominally); گور is the animal 'onager' (not 'grave'); و links the next noun phrase; گرد سوار pairs 'گرد' (dust) with 'سوار' (mounted rider) to mean 'the riders' dust' or 'dust raised by the horsemen'.
segments.s245-g126: برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'. یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / a' used here to introduce دود ('a plume/column of smoke'). زان is a fused preposition/pronoun 'from that' pointing to the place just mentioned, and مرغزار is 'meadow/grassland'—so 'a smoke arose from that meadow.'
segments.s245-g127: بکردار appears as a descriptive/circumstantial element ('in manner/with the quality of'), followed by دریا ('sea') to form a simile or metaphorical description ('with the manner/force of the sea'). زمین بردمید is a compound verbal idea: زمین (the earth/ground) + بردمید (past of 'carry off / seize / lift'), so 'the ground was lifted/seized up.' The phrase is poetically compressed: 'with sea‑like force the earth was lifted.'
segments.s245-g128: کمندافگن is an agent noun 'the lasso‑thrower / lasso‑caster' and, with و گور ('and the onager'), forms the compound subject. شد ناپدید is a light-verb construction: شد ('became') + ناپدید ('vanished/invisible'), so '...became vanished' / 'disappeared.' The sequence says that the lasso‑caster and the onager were made to vanish (were lost from sight).
segments.s245-g281: کنم آفرین بر نیاکان خویش is literally 'I do/present praise upon my ancestors.' کنم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive of کردن used here as 'I give/say'; آفرین functions as a noun/exclamation 'praise/acclamation.' بر + نیاکان 'upon/to the ancestors' marks the beneficiary, and خویش attaches as a possessive phrase 'my own' (خوی 'kinship' + suffix ش = 'my').
segments.s245-g282: گزیده جهاندار و پاکان خویش is an appositive/group of epithets: گزیده 'chosen/selected' modifies جهاندار 'sovereign.' و links to پاکان 'the pure (plural).' خویش again is the possessive element 'my/own' attached to پاکان. Word order is noun phrases in apposition enumerating those praised.
segments.s245-g283: بگویم ترا هر کجا بیژنست — بگویم is the speaker's 'I say/tell' (classical present/subjunctive form); ترا is the enclitic object 'you' (تو + را) meaning 'to you' or 'I tell you.' هر کجا + بیژنست = 'wherever Bijan is,' with بیژنست a fused predicate (proper name + -ست 'is') without a separate copula.
segments.s245-g284: بجام اندرون این مرا روشنست — بجام ('in the right place/at the fitting time') and اندرون ('within/inside') form a locative phrase; این 'this' points to the immediate place or matter. مرا (written here split as م + را) is 'to me' (the dative/accusative first‑person), and روشنست 'is clear' is the predicate meaning 'it is clear to me.' The clause therefore states that the matter is manifest to the speaker here within.
segments.s245-g285: چو بشنید گیو این سخن شاد شد — چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. In classical Persian poetry the verb (بشنید 'heard') can precede the subject (گیو). این سخن 'these words/this speech' is the object; شاد شد 'became happy' is the result clause (شاد 'happy' + شد 'became').
segments.s245-g286: ز تیمار فرزند آزاد شد — ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'because of.' تیمار 'care/solicitude' names the trouble, فرزند 'the son' is the patient, and آزاد شد 'was freed/became free' is the passive/resulting event. Together: 'the son was freed from care/worry.'
segments.s245-g287: بخندید و بر شاه کرد آفرین — بخندید 'he laughed' (past); و 'and' links clauses. بر شاه 'to/upon the king' is the indirect object; کرد آفرین literally 'made praise' (کرد + آفرین) = 'he praised.' This is a conventional poetic way to say 'he expressed praise to the king.'
segments.s245-g288: که بی‌تو مبادا زمان و زمین — که introduces the content/clause quoted or wished. بی‌تو 'without you.' مبادا is an optative/negative wish meaning 'may it not be' or 'let it not happen.' زمان و زمین 'time and earth' are the things wished never to be without the addressee. Grammatically this is a negated optative clause following the praise.
segments.s245-g305: بیامد is a past third‑person singular verb 'he came'. بپوشید is another past verb ('he put on / he wore') with the poetic/prefixal b-; here the two verbs form a simple narrative sequence ('he came and (then) put on'). رومی قبای is the direct object (adjective رومی before noun قبای = 'a Roman robe').
segments.s245-g306: بدان is a compact, poetic deictic meaning 'with that / thereby' (here introducing the purpose/result clause). تا introduces the purpose/result 'so that', and بود is past 'was' (here functioning as the past tense copula: 'so that he might be'). پیش یزدان is a locative/agent phrase 'in the presence of / before God'. بپای (from پاییدن) carries the sense 'to abide / stand' — i.e. 'stand/appear before God.'
segments.s245-g307: خروشید is a past verb 'he/they roared / a cry rose' (خروش as the verb root). پیش جهان‌آفرین is a prepositional phrase 'before the Creator' (پیش = 'in the presence of'). The whole segment states that a loud cry occurred in the Creator's presence.
segments.s245-g308: بخورشید here is presented as an exclamatory/imperative-like or vocative phrase addressing the sun (formally written with b-); بر is the preposition 'upon/on', چند is an interrogative/adverb 'how many / how much', برد is the noun 'gain/victory', and آفرین is the exclamation 'praise / acclamation'. The clause asks rhetorically how much praise or how many acclamations were won.
segments.s245-g309: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. فریادرس is a compound noun 'the rescuer / deliverer (one who answers cries)'. زور is the object 'strength' or 'might'. و simply links clauses 'and', while فریاد خواست is a verb phrase 'he cried out / he sought aid' (خواست is past 'he asked/sought').
segments.s245-g310: از is again the preposition 'from'. آهرمن is the proper name Ahriman (the devil). بدکنش is an epithet 'the ill‑doer / evil‑doer' describing Ahriman. داد here is the noun 'justice' and خواست is the past verb 'he requested / demanded' — together 'he demanded justice'.
segments.s245-g311: خرامان is an adverb/adjectival adverb 'with a stately / proud gait' modifying the verb. ازان جا is the prepositional phrase 'from that place' (ازان = از آن). بیامد is past 'he came'. بگاه is an adverb 'at that moment / at the time', placed adverbially after the verb.
segments.s245-g312: This is a common classical Persian word order for 'placed on the head': بِسر (به‌سر/به سر) = 'on the head' (here written بسر), بر = 'on/upon', نهاد is the past of نهادن 'he placed/put', and آن خجسته کلاه is the direct object 'that auspicious hat'. In natural English: 'he placed that auspicious hat upon his head.'
segments.s245-g337: نگر is an exhortative imperative meaning “consider” or “take heed,” here introducing an admonition. غم نداری functions as a short idiom ‘have no grief’ (نداری used poetically as ‘be without’/‘have no’), and بزندان و بند is a compound locative phrase ‘in prison and in bonds’ (ب + زندان and بند = fetter). Together the clause means ‘take heed: be without sorrow about prison and chains.’
segments.s245-g338: ازان پس is a temporal phrase ‘after that/thereupon’ and که introduces the subordinate explanatory clause. بر جانش = ‘upon his life’ (بر = upon; جان + enclitic ش = his life). نامد گزند is an archaic/perhaps contracted verbal construction meaning ‘no harm came’; here نامد functions as a negative perfect/imperfect (‘did not come’) and گزند = ‘harm, injury.’ The clause gives the reason: afterwards no injury befell him.
segments.s245-g339: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ‘that/for.’ بیژن is the subject. بتوران here has a locative sense ‘in Turan’ (poetic order). ببند اندرست is a classical phrasing meaning ‘is confined/locked up inside’ (ببند = be bound/held; اندرست = ‘is within’), so the clause says Bijan is held in Turan.
segments.s245-g340: زوارش is a partitive/relational phrase formed with the noun زوار plus the possessive enclitic -ش meaning ‘of/around him’ (the exact nuance of زوار is context-dependent here). یکی = ‘one/a certain,’ نامور = ‘renowned,’ and دخترست is the archaic fused form of دختر + است ‘is a maiden.’ The clause means ‘one famed maiden belongs among them/about him.’
segments.s245-g341: ز بس is a poetic intensifier ‘from so much/so great a…’; رنج و سختی و تیمار are three coordinated nouns ‘suffering and hardship and care/tending.’ اوی is an archaic third-person enclitic meaning ‘his/for him,’ tying all those burdens to Bijan. The whole phrase gives the cause: ‘because of so much suffering, hardship, and care for him…’
segments.s245-g342: پر از درد گشتم = ‘I became full of pain’ (پر از = full of; گشتم is the past form of گشتن ‘to become’). من is the emphatic subject pronoun ‘I.’ از کار اوی (‘because of his affair/condition’) gives the cause (از here = ‘because of’). Overall: ‘I became filled with pain on account of his condition.’
segments.s245-g343: بدان سان is a fixed adverbial phrase ‘in that way/thus’ (ب as prefix + دان + سان = ‘to that manner’). گذارد همی روزگار is a poetic way to speak of the movement of time/fate: گذارد (literally ‘placed/caused to pass’) with همی marking continuative aspect and روزگار = ‘time/fate/the days.’ The clause means ‘in that manner the days/time passed on.’
segments.s245-g344: که introduces the resulting clause ‘so that/that.’ هزمان = ‘simultaneously/at the same time.’ بروبر is an adverbial phrase ‘face to face’ or ‘one opposite another.’ بگرید is a classical verbal form (prefix ب- + گرید from گریستن) conveying repeated/habitual or narrative past action ‘would weep/kept weeping’ or simply ‘wept.’ زوار here refers to the mournful/afflicted people (contextual meaning), so the clause says they wept together simultaneously.
segments.s245-g41: همانگه is an adverb meaning “at that moment/immediately.” بدو is the dative pronoun “to him/unto him.” اندر آورد is a past tense clause: آورد (3rd sg. past of آوردن) takes پای (“foot”) as its object; literally “brought/put a foot toward him,” i.e. “stepped toward him.” Word order is emphatic/classical (adverb — indirect object — preposition — verb — object).
segments.s245-g42: بکردار is an epithet/adjective meaning “of good character, well‑bred/virtuous,” used as a title or modifier for the person who follows. باد here is an archaic/classical particle (appearing in older texts) tied into the phrase; the main verb is اندر آمد (آمد in past “came in/approached”), and ز جای is a source phrase “from the place.” Together: “the virtuous one came from his place.”
segments.s245-g43: پذیره شدش is a passive/periphrastic expression: پذیره (reception/acceptance) + شدش (“became/happened to him” with the clitic -ش = “to him”), i.e. “he was admitted/received.” تا introduces the purpose/result clause “so that/in order that”; کند is the classical present/subjunctive 3rd‑person form of کردن used here with a purposive sense “may do/should do,” and خواستار is a predicate noun “one who asks/requests” (i.e. “so that he may apply/ask”).
segments.s245-g44: که here introduces the indirect question/content of the request: “that/asking (about) ...” بیژن is the proper name Bijan. کجا ماند is a past tense clause: کجا “where” + ماند (past of ماندن, “remained/was left”). و joins the second part; چون بود کار is a standard phrase meaning “how was the matter/situation” (چون = “how/as” and بود = past of بودن, “was”), i.e. “and how things stood.”
segments.s245-g45: همی گفت is a formulaic/classical narrative phrase meaning roughly “thus he said” or “he said (then/so)” — همی gives a stylistic/continuous flavor. گرگین is the proper name Gorgin here. بدو is the dative “to him.” ناگهان is an adverb “suddenly.” The order places the speaker and addressee before the sudden adverb for poetic effect.
segments.s245-g46: همانا is an emphatic particle “verily/truly.” بدی is the noun “evil/harm.” ساخت is the past of ساختن “made/engineered” and here means “wrought/contrived” (i.e. devised evil). اندر نهان is a prepositional phrase “in secret/secretly” (اندر = in; نهان = hidden). Together: “truly he contrived evil in secret.”
segments.s245-g47: شوم‌گر is a formulaic/exclamatory phrase used as an oath/curse (literally something like “may I be ill/accursed if ...”), introducing a conditional threat. ببینمش is the first‑person verb + object clitic (ببینم + -ش) and here carries an optative/conditional sense “if I should see him / may I see him (if it happens).” بی before the name بیژنم is classical/poetic and can function as an oathive particle or as a preposition meaning “without”; in oathful expressions it effectively invokes the name (here Bijan) — the line is formulaic and the exact function is archaic; بیژنم is the proper name Bijan with a case ending. The whole segment is a sworn conditional preface to the threatened action.
segments.s245-g48: همانگه “immediately/at that moment.” سرش را is the definite object (“his head”) with the possessive clitic -ش and the object marker را. ز تن بر کردن is an idiomatic verbal phrase: ز + تن = “from the body,” and بر کردن (here بر + کنم) means “to remove/tear off” — together “to cut off from the body.” کنم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کردن used here as a future/intent “I will do.” So: “I will at once cut off his head from his body.”
segments.s245-g49: بیامد is a simple past/inherent perfective verb 'came'. چو here introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. گرگین in this line functions as a name (Gorgin) rather than the adjective 'wolf-like'. مر is an archaic enclitic object pronoun (here marking the speaker/first person as an object) placed before the explicit object او را for emphasis — older Persian often uses such doubled object marking. او را بدید is the finite past 'saw him' with را as the definite/direct-object marker and بدید the past form 'saw'.
segments.s245-g50: پیاده شد is a verbal phrase 'he dismounted / went on foot' where پیاده (on foot) combines with شد (became/did) to indicate the action of getting down. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. پیش او در دوید is 'ran up to him': پیش 'toward/in the presence of', او 'him', در (here a spatial/prepositional element) + دوید 'ran' (simple past). The clause is sequential: after seeing him, he dismounted and immediately ran forward.
segments.s245-g51: همی + گشت is a poetic progressive/continuous marker: همی 'kept/was' with گشت 'became/turned' gives a sense of ongoing action 'kept turning/rolling'. غلتان is a present-participial adjective 'rolling' and بخاک is the prepositional phrase 'to the ground'. اندرا is a proper name (Indra/Andra) used here as the patient/actor of the rolling; word order places the verb phrase before the name for poetic rhythm.
segments.s245-g52: This short phrase strings descriptive nouns/adjectives: شخوده (playful/ungainly/askew) modifies the following Rخان (loose/relaxed, here describing hair or garments). و is 'and'. برهنه سرا is a poetic collocation 'bare-headed' (برهنه 'naked/bare' + سرا used idiomatically for 'head/place' in poetry). Grammatically these are attributive descriptors linked to the subject in the previous segment.
segments.s245-g53: بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'asked' (third person). و links actions: 'and'. گفت is the past of 'say' (here 'said'). ای is the vocative particle 'O!'. گزین سپاه is a vocative phrase 'chosen (one) of the host/army' — گزین functions as an epithet 'the chosen'; سپاه is the noun 'army'. Together they form the addressee phrase of the speech that follows.
segments.s245-g54: This segment strings titles in apposition: سپهدار (a formal title: 'commander'), سالار ('chief, leader') joined by و 'and', then خورشید (literally 'sun' used honorifically) + گاه ('of the age/time') forming an honorific apposition 'sun of the age'. The sequence is a common poetic piling-up of titles addressed to a single person.
segments.s245-g55: پذیره here is a nominal/verb form 'received' / 'the reception' used elliptically to ask who gave reception — i.e. 'who received you'. بدین is a fused prepositional phrase (ب + دین) meaning 'in/at this' (poetic contraction of به این). راه 'road/way' is the locative. چون آمدی here functions temporally 'when/as you came' (چون introducing the subordinate clause). Syntactically this is an interrogative/relative construction asking about the reception on that road at the time of arrival.
segments.s245-g56: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'for/since/that'. با 'with' marks accompaniment. دیدگان is the plural 'eyes' and پر is the adjective 'full (of)' modifying it; ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' and خون 'blood'. آمدی is the past 'you came'. Together the clause explains or justifies the previous question: 'for you came with eyes full of blood.'
segments.s245-g153: This is a reported question: گفت is the reporting verb 'he said'. The clause چه آید مرا is the quoted interrogative (literally 'what comes to me' = 'what will happen to me/what will I gain'), and از کشتنا ('from killing') is a poetic infinitive/nominal (کشتنا) that modifies آید. Word order is: QUESTION (چه آید مرا) + reporting verb (گفت) + adjunct (از کشتنا).
segments.s245-g154: مگر here functions as 'unless' or 'except'. کام 'desire' is the noun, followed by the epithet بدگوهر 'ill‑germ/evil‑gem' qualifying آهرمنا (Ahriman, the Evil One) in apposition; together they mean 'the desire of evil Ahriman'. This is a conditional negative: 'unless (it is) the wish of evil Ahriman.'
segments.s245-g155: This is a rhetorical question: به بیژن 'for Bijan' marks the beneficiary/target, چه سود آید 'what profit will come' uses آید in the sense 'come/happen', and از جان اوی 'from his life' uses اوی as a possessive 'his'. Word order places the beneficiary first for emphasis (به بیژن).
segments.s245-g156: دگرگونه سازیم is a first‑person plural form ('we will/would make/do differently' — سازیم is an older subjunctive/optative/intentional form). درمان 'remedy/cure' is the object, and اوی is the third‑person possessive 'for him/his', so the clause says 'we shall make another sort of remedy for him.'
segments.s245-g157: بباشیم is a hortative/subjunctive 'let us be/remain' (first‑person plural). تا introduces the purpose/temporal clause 'until'. زین here is a demonstrative 'this' (classical usage) modifying سخن 'speech/matter' and نزد شاه 'before the king' marks the scene where it will be presented. The order places the hortative before the subordinating clause.
segments.s245-g158: شود is the present/future auxiliary 'become/come to pass' (passive or intransitive result), آشکارا 'openly/clearly' is an adverb, and ز + گرگین marks 'because of/from Gorgin' (here گرگین is the proper name Gorgin). گناه 'fault/blame' is the noun whose revelation is asserted. Together: 'and Gorgin's guilt will be revealed.'
segments.s245-g159: This clause uses an impersonal/verbal noun construction: ازو 'from him' + کین کشیدن 'to draw/make vengeance' (an idiom meaning 'to take revenge on him') is treated as the act, followed by بسی کار نیست 'is not a small/easy affair' (بسی = 'much/considerable', کار نیست = 'is not a (simple) task'). The verb نیست negates the clause.
segments.s245-g160: سنان 'spear' is the noun; the sequence مرا را here represents possession/association with the speaker (mora/ra is a clitic/object/topic marking in older spelling — the glossing here is irregular), and پیش دیوار 'in front of the wall' is a locative phrase. The final نیست negates possession or availability: 'I have no spear before the wall / at hand.'
segments.s245-g257: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb meaning ‘he commanded/said’ and comes first in the classical V–S order; خسرو is the subject (the king’s name) that follows the verb; بپولادگر names the addressee/agent (‘the armorer’) and functions as the indirect object or vocative to whom the order is given.
segments.s245-g258: که introduces the content/purpose of the command ('that/to so that'); بندگران and مسمارسر are the objects of the command (types of fastenings/weapons) joined by و. ساز here is a compact poetic nominal form related to making/means (the phrase as a whole should be read as an instruction to produce these items).
segments.s245-g259: هم اندر زمان is a temporal/contrastive preface ('also/at once, at that time'); پای is the body-part noun 'foot(–s)'; کردش is a light verb with the enclitic object -ش 'him' (literally 'made/did to him'); ببند at the end is an imperative addressed to the workmen — 'bind' or 'fasten'. The sequence follows classical Persian order where adverbials precede the action and the imperative closes the clause.
segments.s245-g260: که here gives the purpose/result ('so that'); از بند is an ablative phrase 'from/by the fetter'; گیرد is a verbal form indicating the result (here read as 'be taken/affected' — 'may be fettered/affected by the bond'); بداندیش is the target description 'the ill‑minded/evil‑minded one'; پند means 'admonition/lesson'. Together the clause gives the reason: to teach the ill‑minded a lesson by fettering him.
segments.s245-g261: بگیو is an archaic vocative/imperative form 'say!' or 'tell (so)' and opens the reported utterance; آنگهی 'then/thereupon' places the reporting temporally; گفت is the narrative past 'he said' (here used to introduce direct speech); بازآر هوش is a verbal phrase 'bring back (بازآر) (his) senses/awareness (هوش)' and is the content of the spoken command.
segments.s245-g262: بجویش is a compound verb form meaning 'seek/search for him/it' (the object refers back to the person whose senses were to be restored); بهر جای 'for every place' = 'in every place'; و هر سو بکوش links two imperatives/actions: 'and in every direction strive/attempt' (بکوش is an imperative 'strive'). The clause lists the orders for an exhaustive search.
segments.s245-g263: من اکنون is the explicit first‑person subject plus temporal adverb 'I now'; ز هر سو = 'from every side' (poetic preposition ز = 'from'); فراوان سپاه means 'a plentiful army' (fراوان modifies سپاه). The phrase sets up the speaker's resolution to mobilize forces.
segments.s245-g264: فرستم is first‑person present/future 'I send/dispatch' and governs the following purpose clause; بجویم is a subjunctive 'to seek/search for' coordinated with the sending (i.e. 'I will send ... to seek'); بهر جا نگاه combines 'for/to every place' and نگاه (lit. 'gaze/view') to mean 'to look/search in every place.' The two verbs express sending troops with the explicit task of searching everywhere.
segments.s245-g209: This short clause opens with بِکین (here 'بِ کین' = 'with/for vengeance'), an adverbial phrase giving motive. سیاوش (Siyavash) is simply the addressee/name placed after that phrase. کشم here is a first‑person verb form (classical/poetic word‑order places the verb before or after its logical object) meaning 'I will draw/lead/slay' depending on context; لشکرا is the accusative/indefinite 'army/armies' (لشکر with an old‑case ending). Together the clause reads literally 'With vengeance, Siyavash, I (will) [do] army'.
segments.s245-g210: بپیلان is a compact/poetic form indicating motion or accompaniment with elephants (literally 'with/onto elephants'); سرآرم is a first‑person present/future sense 'I will raise my head / I will show myself / I will appear' (the compound سر + آمدن/آرم used figuratively). از آن کشورا is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of that country' (از = from, آن = that, کشور + old plural/oblique ending). The whole clause describes the manner/place of the speaker's appearance.
segments.s245-g211: بدان here functions as a demonstrative/locative compound roughly 'in/with that' (poetic fusion of prepositional/pointer elements). کینه is the noun 'grudge/hatred'. اندر بود is the older Persian locative + past copula construction 'was in/within'. بیژنا is the name or epithet placed at the end as the experiencer/subject (classical Persian often puts the subject after the predicate). The clause thus means 'that grudge was in Bijna'.
segments.s245-g212: همی is a habitual/stative adverb 'continually/always'. رزم is the object 'battle/war' and جوید is the present/habitual verb 'seeks/wishes for' (classical present meaning 'keeps seeking'). چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and آهرمنا is Ahriman (the Evil One) used as a comparison to stress his warlike, evil eagerness. Word order is canonical poetic Persian: adverb — object — verb — simile phrase.
segments.s245-g213: تو دل را places 'your heart' as the direct object with the object marker را (literal order 'you heart-OBJ'); بدین کار is a prepositional/phrastic unit 'by/with this matter' (بدین = 'in/with this', کار = 'affair'). غمگین مدار is an imperative prohibition 'do not make (it) sad' — مدار is used here as a negative‑imperative form (poetic/archaic) meaning 'do not cause/allow'. The entire clause is a direct admonition: 'Do not sadden your heart over this.'
segments.s245-g214: من این را is the emphatic topicaliser 'I (for my part), this (object)'. همانا is an emphatic adverb 'truly/indeed'. بسم here is an adverb/adjective meaning 'enough/it suffices' and خواستار is a predicative adjective 'desirous/one who wants'. Together they form a compact assertion 'I, indeed, am desirous of this (and it suffices)' — a poetic way of saying 'I myself want this.'
segments.s245-g215: بشد is a past/resultative verb 'he became/it came to be' (poetic past). گیو (Giv) is the proper name placed after the verb (verb–subject order is common in poetry). یکدل is an adjective 'of one heart / single‑minded', and پر اندوه و درد is a predicative phrase 'full of sorrow and pain' (پر = 'full (of)' followed by the nouns اندوه and درد). The clause says that Giv became steadfast yet overwhelmed with grief.
segments.s245-g216: This is a verbless descriptive follow‑up: دو دیده 'two eyes' (subject), پر از آب 'full of water' (i.e. full of tears) and رخساره زرد 'and (his) face/complextion (was) yellow/pale'. Classical Persian often omits the copula in such descriptive lines; the predicates follow the subject and are linked by و 'and'.
segments.s245-g185: بدآگاهی آورد is a compact verbal phrase: بدآگاهی ('bad news / misapprehension') is the noun-object and آورد is the past tense 3rd‑person singular of آوردن, here meaning 'brought' or 'reported'. از پور من places the theme after the verb in the poetic order: از means 'about / concerning' and پور من = 'my son' (پور = 'son', من = 'my/I' as possessor).
segments.s245-g186: ازان نامور پاک دستور من is a continuation: ازان ('from/of that') links back to the preceding report; نامور ('renowned') and پاک ('pure, noble') are adjectival modifiers; دستور here means 'retinue/command/force' or 'order' and من is the possessive 'my' (literally 'of me'). Word order is adjective(s) + noun + possessor, typical in poetry.
segments.s245-g187: یکی اسب دیدم نگونسار زین: یکی ('a certain') introduces an indefinite; اسب is the noun 'horse' and دیدم is the 1st‑person past 'I saw'. نگونسار ('mournful/woeful') modifies the horse, and زین ('saddle') is appended to indicate the horse is saddled — poetic word order places modifiers and attributes after the verb.
segments.s245-g188: ز بیژن نشانی ندارد جزین is a negative predication: ز (poetic 'of / pertaining to') بیژن ('Bijan') sets the reference; نشانی ('mark, trace') is the noun and ندارد is the present 3rd‑person negative 'does not have'. جزین ('except this') limits the negation. Overall: 'It has no mark/trace of Bijan except this.'
segments.s245-g189: اگر داد بیند بدین کار ما: اگر is the conditional 'if'. داد here means 'justice' and بیند is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive of دیدن; together داد بیند is an idiomatic way to speak of 'justice being seen/done' (i.e. 'if justice is done'). بدین is the compound prepositional form (ب + دین) meaning 'in/for this', and کار ما = 'our affair / this matter of ours'.
segments.s245-g190: یکی بنگرد ژرف سالار ما: یکی here functions as 'once' / 'one (time)' or 'at least one', and بنگرد is the optative/subjunctive 3rd‑person form 'may (he) look / behold'. ژرف ('deep, profound') qualifies سالار ('lord, commander'), and ما is the possessive 'our' (we). The clause is a wish: 'may our deep lord look once.'
segments.s245-g191: ز گرگین دهد داد من شهریار: شهریار ('O king') is vocative; the speaker petitions the king. ز گرگین here means 'against / concerning Gurgin' (Gurgin is a proper name), دهد is a 3rd‑person present/optative of دادن 'may (you) give / grant', and داد من = 'my justice' or 'justice for me'. The line reads as an appeal: 'O king, grant me justice against Gurgin.'
segments.s245-g192: کزو گشتم اندر جهان خاکسار: کزو ('from whom / of whom') refers back to Gurgin; گشتم is the past 1st‑person 'I became' or 'I was made'; اندر جهان = 'in the world'; خاکسار is an adjective/noun meaning 'humbled, contrite, abased (one who is lowly)'. Literally: 'from him I have become abased in the world.'
segments.s245-g89: This is an interrogative clause in the past tense addressed to “you” (تو). The verb یافتی is second-person singular past “you found”; چون before the verb functions as the question word “how.” The noun phrase این مرده‌ری اسب is the object: مرده‌ری (an archaic/poetic noun meaning the state of death/rottenness attached to اسب) follows the demonstrative این.
segments.s245-g90: ز is the poetic preposition “from,” followed by the name بیژن as the source. کجا is the interrogative “where.” روی is the noun “face” (used in the idiom روی برتافتن = “to turn one’s face away”), and برتافتی is a past 2nd-sg form meaning “you turned away” (the exact lemma isn’t in the provided senses, so the listed senses don’t match this verbal usage).
segments.s245-g91: بدو is a dative/clitic phrase “to him” (literally ‘unto him’), placed before the verb; گفت here is the speech verb ‘said’ (the provided senses list does not include this verbal sense). گرگین is the speaker’s name (Gorgin). که introduces the commanded content (‘that’). بازآر is an imperative or causative ‘bring back/restore’ and takes هوش (“sense, awareness”) as its object, so بازآر هوش = “restore your senses.”
segments.s245-g92: This contains two coordinated imperatives. سخن بشنو = ‘hear/listen to the speech/words’ (سخن object, بشنو imperative “listen”). و simply joins the commands. پهن modifies the verb that follows as an adverbial/manner word “wide,” and بگشای (imperative of گشودن) governs گوش as its object: پهن بگشای گوش = “open (your) ear wide.” Word order is verb-final for objects but imperatives allow still fronting of manner (پهن) before the verb.
segments.s245-g93: که begins reported/explanatory content (‘that’/‘how’); این کار چون بود is an embedded question/ clause meaning ‘how this matter was’ (چون = ‘how’, بود = past ‘was’). The coordination و links the parallel clause کردار چون = ‘how the deed/conduct (کردار) was’ or ‘how the action unfolded.’ Note the classical parallelism: two ‘چون’ clauses asking for description of event and conduct.
segments.s245-g94: بدان here is the imperative formed from ب + دان (“be‑/to‑ + know”) and functions as ‘know this’ or ‘be aware.’ بیشه is the locative object ‘in that thicket,’ introduced after بدان to specify where. با خوک پیکار is a prepositional/agent phrase ‘the fight with the boar’ (با = ‘with’, خوک = ‘boar/pig’, پیکار = ‘battle’). The final چون again asks ‘how’: altogether ‘know how in that thicket the fight with the boar took place.’
segments.s245-g95: بدان is the imperative ‘know this’ (ب prefix + دان ‘to know’) addressing the addressees; پهلوانا is a vocative plural ‘O champions/strongmen.’ و connects the clauses. آگاه باش is an admonition ‘be aware’ or ‘be vigilant,’ with باش the imperative of بودن used as a command.
segments.s245-g96: همیشه is an adverb ‘always.’ فروزنده is an adjective/epithet ‘illuminating, shining’ modifying the implied subject; گاه here means ‘presence/occasion’ (used poetically as a nominal partner) and باش is again the imperative ‘be’ — together, ‘always be a shining presence’ or ‘ever be bright.’
segments.s245-g81: ز + proper name is a terse, poetic way to indicate the agent — here ‘ز گرگین’ functions like ‘it was Gorgin’ / ‘from Gorgin’. پس آنگه are sequence adverbs (‘then’, ‘afterwards’) emphasizing the next action. سخن بازجست is an idiom: سخن (speech) with the past verb بازجست (3rd sg.) yields ‘resumed/began speaking again’; بازجست is a past tense verb with the verbal prefix باز- (‘again’). Word order is flexible and idiomatic: agent phrase + temporal adverbs + object/nominal (سخن) + verb.
segments.s245-g82: که introduces a subordinate (here an indirect question) ‘that/asking’. چون is the interrogative ‘how’. بود is the past 3rd-person of ‘to be’ (reporting the state). خود here intensifies or makes ‘the very’ (the essence of) the following noun. روزگار is the noun ‘time/fate/the times’. از نخست is a prepositional phrase ‘from the first / from the beginning’. The clause order is classical Persian: complementizer + interrogative + verb + intensified subject + temporal phrase.
segments.s245-g83: زمانه (here: fate/the workings of the times) is the subject. بجایش is a fused form of به جایِش (‘in his place’ / ‘in his stead’) functioning adverbially; its surface sense lists did not list the exact reading, so it is treated as the idiom ‘in his stead’. کسی is the selected/appointed person (object), and برگزید is the past 3rd-person verb ‘chose/selected’. The clause structure places the adverbial (بجایش) between subject and object, which classical Persian allows.
segments.s245-g84: وگر is a conditional/concessive ‘if / otherwise’. خود is an emphatic/reflexive particle ‘himself’. ز چشم تو is a prepositional phrase literally ‘from your eye’ i.e. ‘to your sight’; ز functions as the preposition ‘from/of’. شد is the past copula/verb ‘became’, and ناپدید is the resultative adjective ‘invisible/vanished’. Together شد ناپدید = ‘became unseen/invisible’. The clause expresses the counterfactual result that would have occurred absent the prior clause.
segments.s245-g85: ز بدها چه آمد is an idiomatic interrogative: ز (poetic ‘from’) + بدها (pl. ‘evils/misfortunes’) + چه آمد (‘what came/happened’), meaning ‘what misfortunes befell him’. مر is a dative clitic ‘to me’ (tell me), followed by او را (object marker on ‘him’) and the imperative/archaic imperative form بگوی (‘tell’). Here بگوی is an older/poetic imperative of گفتن; the provided noun-like senses do not match the verbal imperative, so it is treated as the verb ‘tell/say’. Word order places the request (‘tell me’) after the question phrase.
segments.s245-g86: چه is the interrogative ‘what’. افگند is the past tense of افکندن ‘to throw/cast’. بند here means ‘fetter / bond’ (a figurative chain). سپهرش = سپهر + ­-ش (‘the heavens’ or ‘the sky’ with a possessive suffix referring back to the subject/context), so بند سپهرش = ‘the heavens’ fetter’. بروی functions as a locative/on-phrase ‘upon him / on his face’ (poetic contraction of به روی); the lemma senses shown for بروی as motion verbs do not reflect this prepositional use, so it is treated as the locative adposition meaning ‘on/upon’. The whole phrase asks what celestial chain was cast upon him.
segments.s245-g87: چه is the interrogative ‘what’. دیو is the subject ‘demon/fiend’. آمدش is the past verb ‘came’ with the enclitic -ش indicating ‘to him’ (i.e., ‘came to him’). پیش در مرغزار is a locative phrase ‘before/in front (of him) in the meadow’; پیش here carries the sense ‘in the presence of / before’ and در مرغزار specifies the place. Classical Persian freely allows the verb with clitic to attach and the locatives to follow.
segments.s245-g88: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause (‘which/that’). او را marks the person affected (‘him’ as definite object). تبه کرد is a clause meaning ‘did harm to / ruined’, where تبه is a noun/adjective meaning ‘evil/harm’ used with کرد ‘did’. و برگشت کار is coordinated (‘and’) + برگشت (a noun/verb ‘return/reversal’) + کار (‘the affair’), idiomatically meaning ‘and the affair was turned back/reversed’ — i.e., his fortunes were changed. Word order and the use of کرد make this a compact narrative cause-and-effect clause.
segments.s245-g225: وزین روی گرگین شوریده رفت is an adjectival phrase + verb: وزین is an adjective ('dignified/weighty') modifying روی (‘face’); the following adjectives گرگین ('wolf-like') and شوریده ('distraught') further describe روی. رفت is simple past, 3rd person singular ('he went'). The whole string therefore means ‘he went with that [specified] face’. Note in poetry adjectives can appear before or after the noun; here one adjective precedes the noun (وزین روی) and two follow it (گرگین شوریده).
segments.s245-g226: بنزدیک is an adverbial/prepositional expression meaning 'near' or 'close at hand' and governs the following locative phrase. ایوان درگاه تفت is a genitive/possessive chain: ایوان ('iwan/portico') followed by درگاه ('threshold/portal') which is in turn specified by تفت (here treated as a proper name/place or a final specifier). Word order is head noun → modifier(s), a normal poetic arrangement for a place-name phrase.
segments.s245-g227: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. در پیش کیخسرو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before Khosrow' (در = 'in', پیش = 'before/in the presence of', KiKsro is the name). آمد is the past-tense verb 'he came'. The following noun زمین is placed here but functions as the object of the next verb (a flexible poetic word order: temporal clause + object anticipates the action).
segments.s245-g228: ببوسید is past tense 'he kissed' (3rd sg.). The conjunction و links the two acts. بر with کرد آفرین means 'toward/to the king he made/sang “afarin”'; here بر + شاه gives the indirect object/target ('to the king') and کرد آفرین is a light-verb construction where کرد ('made/did/uttered') takes the noun آفرین ('acclamation/bravo') as its complement—i.e. 'he praised/cried Bravo to the king.'
segments.s245-g229: چو is 'when/as'. الماس qualifies the following noun phrase as a simile or likeness ('like diamonds'); دندانهای گراز is a possessive/genitive construction 'the teeth (tusks) of the boar' (head noun دندانهای followed by its possessor گراز). This whole phrase therefore reads 'when the boar's teeth, like diamonds...'.
segments.s245-g230: بر تخت بنهاد is a verb phrase: بر تخت ('upon the throne') is the locative complement and بنهاد is the past tense verb 'he placed/put' (classical بنهاد = 'he set/established/placed'). The conjunction و links the following verb; بردش is an older/contracted past form with the enclitic -ش (third-person object 'it'), i.e. 'he took/carried it' (here the exact verbal lemma/nuance is archaic or compressed in the manuscript). نماز follows as a noun indicating the ritual/ceremonial context or purpose; depending on reading it can mean 'he took it as a trophy/for the ceremony' or 'he removed it and offered (or accompanied) it with prayer.' The enclitic -ش on بردش marks the object 'it'.
segments.s245-g231: که introduces a wished-for clause; خسرو is the subject 'Khosrow'. بهر کار is a prepositional/benefactive phrase 'for every work/affair' (بهر = 'for/toward' + کار = 'task/affair'). پیروز is a predicative adjective 'victorious'. باد here is the optative particle 'may be' (classical subjunctive/wish). The literal order is 'that Khosrow for/at affairs victorious may be' → 'May Khosrow be victorious in his endeavours.'
segments.s245-g232: همه modifies روزگارش ('all his days/whole of his time' — روزگار with possessive suffix ـش). چو is the comparative/simile 'as/like', نوروز is the proper noun 'Nowruz' (symbol of renewal/joy). The final باد is again the optative 'may it be'. The line therefore expresses a wish: 'May all his days be like Nowruz.'
segments.s245-g169: نباشد is a negated present/subjunctive form of بودن used here to mean 'there will not be / you shall not have'; the 2nd-person enclitic ترا (ت + را) follows the verb as its object, so the verb precedes its object. بیش ازین (= بیش از این) means 'any more / more than this' with از introducing the comparison and ین (= این) meaning 'this'. دستگاه is a poetic word for 'court/establishment' or 'standing/position' in a courtly context.
segments.s245-g170: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. من is the first-person pronoun placed before the verb for emphasis. ببینم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/optative of دیدن used here like 'can I / might I see' (classical/polite possibility). یکی modifies روی as 'even one / a single', and روی شاه = 'the king's face' (the ezāfe linking is omitted in poetry).
segments.s245-g171: پس آنگه is a double temporal adverb ('then / in that case'). بخواهم is 1st-person present/subjunctive of خواستن used here as 'I will ask/demand' or 'I shall seek'. ز (poetic) introduces the source/agent 'from you' (تو). کین خوی + ش is 'my/one's vengeance' formed by خوی 'own/kin/possession' plus the possessive suffix ش; the two parts together express the speaker's (or the referenced person's) vengeful claim.
segments.s245-g172: ز بهر is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'for the sake/sake of'. گرامی 'honored' modifies جهانبین, an epithet literally 'world-seer' (here a noble/title). خوی + ش again forms a possessive/kinship expression ('his/my kinsman' in context), so the whole phrase means 'for the sake of my honored world-seeing kinsman.'
segments.s245-g173: وز here functions as an archaic connective ('and/from there') leading into the action; آنجا 'there' marks the place; بیامد is the past tense 'he came'; بنزدیک (بِـ + نزدِیک) means 'near' or 'close to', followed by شاه 'the king' as the goal. Word order places place/adverbial before the verb in this poetic line.
segments.s245-g174: دو دیده is 'two eyes'. پر از خون is a fixed adjective phrase 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)'). و links the next nominal phrase; دل کینه‌خواه is 'a heart that is vengeful' or 'a vengeful heart' where کینه‌خواه is a compound meaning 'seeking/holding vengeance' (an avenger or vengeful). The noun phrases are juxtaposed without a copula, a common poetic device.
segments.s245-g175: برو here indicates movement ('he went' / 'he went off') followed by آفرین کرد, where آفرین is an exclamation/praise and کرد is the past verb 'he made/uttered' (i.e. 'he cried out praise'). کای شهریار is a vocative phrase 'O Kay, king'. The sequence is narrational: subject/action + the words uttered.
segments.s245-g176: همیشه جهان را بشادی گذار is a jussive/wishful construction: همیشه 'always', جهان را (object-marked 'the world'), بشادی 'in joy' (adverbial), and گذار (here functioning as a verb meaning 'cause/let pass' or 'let be') expresses a wish or command ('may you make the world always joyful' / 'let the world ever be in joy'). Word order places the temporal adverb before the object and the verb at the end, as is common in Persian.
segments.s245-g329: بچاهی is the fused locative 'به چاهی' = 'in/into a well' (poetic locative). ببسته is a verbal adjective/past participle 'fastened/tied' describing the state; ببند is an imperative/jussive-form verb 'shut/close' (here rendering the action); گران is an adjective 'heavy/tightly' modifying how he was bound/closed. The phrase is poetic and elliptical: locative → participle → verb → manner adverb/adjective.
segments.s245-g330: ز سختی is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of hardship' giving cause. همی + جست is an older imperfect/habitual construction: همی marks continuous or repeated action and جست (past of جستن) here means 'sprang/leapt' (sense 0) describing repeated occurrence. مرگ is the subject 'death' and اندرَان is a locative adverb 'therein/there' linking back to the well.
segments.s245-g331: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' introducing a person. دختری is 'a maiden/girl' (indefinite). از نژاد کیان is a genitive phrase 'of the lineage/house of the Kians' (از indicating origin, نژاد = lineage, کیان = the Kayan royal house). Word order is noun phrase → prepositional origin phrase.
segments.s245-g332: ز بهر is a purposive phrase 'for/for the sake of' (ز + بهر = 'for'). زوارش is 'the folds/wrinkles of it/her' (زوار = folds; -ش is the 3rd-person possessive suffix). ببسته is a past participle 'bound/tied', here describing her condition, and میان is a locative/adverb 'in the middle/among'. Together they mean she was bound and put amid the folds (poetic phrasing for being wrapped/secured).
segments.s245-g333: سوی + proper name (گیو) is a directional phrase 'toward Giv'. کرد is the general past verb 'did/made' used here with سوی to mean 'turned toward'. آنگهی is a temporal connector 'then'. روی شاه is a possessive/intransitive phrasing 'the king's face' (poetic order: noun 'face' followed by possessor 'king'): literally 'the king's visage'.
segments.s245-g334: بخندید is the past tense 'laughed/smiled'. و is the coordinating 'and'. رخشنده is an adjective 'radiant/bright-faced' and شد is the past of شدن 'became' (here copular/auxiliary). پیشگاه is a locative/relational noun 'in the presence (of the court)/before (the sovereign)', functioning adverbially to set the scene.
segments.s245-g335: که here introduces a causal/explanatory clause 'since/because'. زندست is an archaic/polite present 'is alive' (زند + است → زندست). بیژن is the personal name Bijan. دلت is دل + -ت (your heart), a possessive construction. شاد دار is a periphrastic imperative: شاد (happy) + دار (imperative of داشتن 'keep/hold') meaning 'keep (your) heart joyful' — note that the lemma list for دار provided noun senses, but here دار is the verbal imperative 'keep', so that verbal sense is not among the listed noun senses.
segments.s245-g336: ز هر بد تن is a protective phrase 'from every bad/ill body' meaning 'from every bodily harm' (ز = 'from', هر = 'every', بد = 'bad/harmful', تن = 'body'). مهتر آزاد دار is an imperative clause: مهتر (the chief/important man) is the person to be protected, آزاد (free) is the predicative adjective, and دار is again the imperative 'keep/hold' (i.e. 'keep the chief free'). As above, دار functions as a verb here, so its verbal sense is not listed in the provided noun senses.

sentences.s245-s101: When Khosrow heard the words from Giv, he said to him: "Do not worry and do not lament."
sentences.s245-s102: Be glad that Bijan is alive; remain hopeful concerning the lost child.
sentences.s245-s103: For I have now heard this from the mōbeds, from the renowned, clear‑hearted wise men.
sentences.s245-s104: That I will, with Iran's horsemen, go to fight toward the city of Turan without delay.
sentences.s245-s65: I pursued after him across every plain and mountain, for my steed became weary from galloping.
sentences.s245-s66: I saw no sign of Bijan anywhere except this horse and this saddle, dragged here from behind.
sentences.s245-s67: My heart became full of fire from my concern for him, for he was engaged in battle with the onager.
sentences.s245-s68: I remained for a long time on that meadow; continually I sought him in every direction.
sentences.s245-s89: Anoushe Jahandar, O noble sovereign — do you not see what has befallen me?
sentences.s245-s90: I was one son of the world — a young man; night and day I lived in double youth.
sentences.s245-s91: For his life — full of fear I wept; from the pain of his separation I was consumed (burned).
sentences.s245-s92: Now came — O king Gorgin — from the road: a tongue full of acquired (things) and a soul full of sin.
sentences.s245-s73: When he saw that Giv, the son, was lost, he perceived the speech to be thus tainted.
sentences.s245-s74: Ahriman carried away Giv’s heart from its place; he wished to make one fall from his feet (i.e. to overthrow someone).
sentences.s245-s75: He would seek from him a son for vengeance; but if however much good comes to him from this...
sentences.s245-s76: Then he pondered and resolved to look into them; no light (no clarity) appeared.
sentences.s245-s5: He went through the whole thicket and saw no one; nor did he hear the cry of the birds there.
sentences.s245-s6: He kept circling that meadow, continually seeking a companion there.
sentences.s245-s7: From afar he saw, one by one, Bijan's horse, which came forth from those meadows.
sentences.s245-s8: The bridle was broken and the saddle overturned; it lay fallen on the spot, sorrowful.
sentences.s245-s1: For a week Gorgin, the lamb-bearer, kept watch, and Bijan did not return to his place.
sentences.s245-s2: From every quarter the searchers began to look, and they took to washing Rokhān with bloody water.
sentences.s245-s3: Remorse came to him for that deed of his, because he had so ill-set dogs upon his friend.
sentences.s245-s4: The Arabs advanced as far as that festive place where Bijan and Giv had lost the way.
sentences.s245-s69: I turned back from him, thus hopeless — for it was a living grave and a white demon.
sentences.s245-s70: When Giv heard this speech — alert — he perceived where the affair was ruined.
sentences.s245-s71: He stared fixedly throughout at Gorgin’s words; his eyes continually saw his face grow dark.
sentences.s245-s72: His countenance went pale from fear of the king’s commander; his speech trembled, and his heart was full of sin.
sentences.s245-s49: We left here to fight the boar and reached near Arman Faraz.
sentences.s245-s50: We saw a certain grove, its trees cut short like stumps — a low pasture.
sentences.s245-s51: Every place has become the boar’s lair; the whole city of Arman, because of that, was in convulsion (tetanus/panic).
sentences.s245-s52: When we raised our spears for battle, we entered the grove and raised a cry within.
sentences.s245-s9: He realized whose affair was ruined; such a thing does not befall Iran in these days.
sentences.s245-s10: If there are gallows, pits, or fetters, harm has come to him from Afrasiab.
sentences.s245-s11: The snare cast him in; he turned his face away, repentant of what he had done and his heart seeking a companion.
sentences.s245-s12: From that meadow Bijan drove his horse, brought it into the tent, and stayed a day.
sentences.s245-s157: A certain one set a cup upon his palm; in it, like wine, the seven countries appeared.
sentences.s245-s158: The lofty heaven revealed the time and the signs; it made everything evident — what, how, and how many.
sentences.s245-s159: From the moonlike beauty inside the cup to the lamb — every painted figure was shown wholly.
sentences.s245-s160: Like Kayvân, Bahram, Nâhid and the Lion; like the Sun and Tir above and the Moon below.
sentences.s245-s97: The king was filled with sorrow because of the pain in Giv's heart; he grew angry and put on a fortunate cap.
sentences.s245-s98: The king's countenance at that moment became colorless; his heart grew tight from concern for Bijan.
sentences.s245-s99: Then Gorgin said to Giv, 'What did he say? What can he say? Where has any good mate remained?'
sentences.s245-s100: After Gorgin's speech, then Giv spoke with Khosrow about Pour Niu.
sentences.s245-s53: Boars came in—mountain‑formed; they did not come one by one, but in troops taking every place.
sentences.s245-s54: We made war like a lion; day dawned and renown came, and the heart was sated with battle.
sentences.s245-s55: When we hurled the elephants at one another, we tore out their tusks with spikes.
sentences.s245-s56: And from there we set our course toward the wastes, rejoicing all the way and seeking spoils.
sentences.s245-s145: May the lofty heavens be to your desire; may no harm ever befall your life.
sentences.s245-s146: May a gift of goodness and praise be yours; for hat and jewel befit you.
sentences.s245-s147: When Giv left Khosrow’s presence, horsemen were dispatched in every direction.
sentences.s245-s148: They began searching the whole world, to see if they could find any fitting trace of him.
sentences.s245-s149: They handed over the whole city, Arman, and the Turanians, and no sign came from Bijan.
sentences.s245-s150: When the auspicious Nowruz rose aloft, to that bright cup a supplication came to him.
sentences.s245-s151: The champion's heart came full of hope for the sake of the son, the hunchbacked Nuvan.
sentences.s245-s152: When Khosrow saw Giv's face withered, he saw that his heart was torn and inwardly distressed.
sentences.s245-s37: If my child becomes separated from me, I consider it permissible if he breaks my bond.
sentences.s245-s38: May my soul go to that place of the righteous, free of earthly ties; you know better than I the pain of my heart.
sentences.s245-s39: To me, he himself was the whole world and that was enough; what a remover of sorrow and what a rescuer in cries he was.
sentences.s245-s40: Now ill fortune has torn him away from me; I remain thus afflicted in the world.
sentences.s245-s33: When Gorgin's words reached his ear, he fell from the horse and his consciousness left him.
sentences.s245-s34: His head sank into the earth and vanished; all his garments were torn at the sides.
sentences.s245-s35: He kept pulling out the hair from his head and beard cleanly; tumultuously he threw dust upon his head.
